1:1.text: Year of the World 2990, Year before Christ 1014.; 3 Kings iii.

1:5.text: Sought it: went (Menochius) to offer sacrifice, (Tirinus) in the second year of his reign, the year before Christ 1013.

1:6.text: Victims. Hebrew, "holocausts," either with his own hand, or by the ministry of the priests. See 1 Kings 13:12.

1:7.text: Night, after the victims had been offered. On this occasion, Solomon made this prayer, and was heard, 3 Kings 3:5.

1:10.text: People, as a king ought to do.

1:13.text: Before. Hebrew and Septuagint, "from before the tabernacle," which was at Gabaon. There was another at Jerusalem, where (Haydock) Solomon rendered homage to the Lord.

1:14.text: Chariots. See 3 Kings 4:26. No king of Israel kept such a multitude of horses.

1:15.text: Sycamores are trees of small value, and not good for building.

1:16.text: Coa may signify either some city, (Mariana) or an "emporium," where things are sold, as kuch means "a collection." (Tirinus) (3 Kings 10:28.) Neglecting the points of the Masorets, we may well understand Coa, a city of Egypt, or an island of the Mediterranean. The Masorets read Mikve, instead of Micoa.

2:1.text: Himself, worthy of his great empire. He deemed that which David had built too mean, though that pious king had been ashamed to dwell in such a magnificent palace, while the ark of God was under skins, 2 Kings 7:2.

2:2.text: Hew. The stones were made ready for use, as well as the wood, before it was brought to the temple, 3 Kings 6:7.
Six. Only three are mentioned [in] 3 Kings 5:16.
But three hundred overseers of higher order are here included.

2:6.text: Before him. For this purpose do I design to build.
Temples are more for our use than for God's, as none can be worthy of him.

2:7.text: Purple. Hebrew argevan, (Haydock) a Chaldean word, of the same import as argueman, in Exodus.
Scarlet and blue were also species of purple. The finest sort was found between Tyre and Carmel. See Vitruvius 7:13.

2:8.text: Pine. Hebrew algum, which rather denotes a species of fir, than the juniper-tree; though the domestic kind was tall, and used in edifices.
Arceuthina, "juniper," is taken from the Septuagint.

2:10.text: Barley and wine are not specified [in] 3 Kings 5:11.
Measures, like the Roman amphora, contained 960 ozs.
Hebrew has, "batim," in both places; but [in] 3 Kings we find, "twenty cores of oil." The satum, "measure," was only one-third of the bath or epha.

2:13.text: Father. Hebrew Abi, is considered by some as the surname of Hiram.

But he might have that title in consideration of his great skill, as Solomon gives it him, 2 Paralipomenon 4:16. We use master in the same sense. Septuagint have, "servant," (Greek: paida) except the Roman edition, which agrees with the Hebrew and reads, Greek: patera.
Protestants, "of Huram, my father's;" (Haydock) supply servant, or architect.

2:14.text: Dan, the city, as the widow as of the tribe of Nephthali.
Whose. Hebrew, "and his father," (Haydock) or "this Abi." (Calmet) See 3 Kings 7:14.
Silk. Hebrew, "byssus," which is the silk extracted from a fish, and not the fine linen of Egypt, or cotton which as sometimes this appellation, 1 Paralipomenon 15:27.
My lord, a term of civility.

2:16.text: Floats. So the Septuagint well express the Hebrew raphsodoth, which seems to be borrowed from the Greek Rapsodia, which denotes a collection of verses (Calmet) and was applied to Homer's poems, before they were collected. (Aelian 13:14.) Joppe was a port much used, (Calmet) though dangerous.

2:17.text: Had made, at the commencement of Solomon's reign, when David put such immense treasures into his hands. The second list was taken when the temple was begun. The proselytes were the remnants of the nations of the natives of Chanaan. The Jews foolishly pretend, (Calmet) that no strangers were allowed to embrace the law of Moses, under David and Solomon, for fear lest they might be influenced by self-interest rather than by the love of religion.

2:18.text: Six. We read three, 3 Kings 5:16.: people who where strangers, as the Israelites were not forced to work, 2 Paralipomenon 8:9.

3:1.text: Moria, which signifies vision; (Calmet) the place pointed out to Abraham, (Genesis 22:2.; St. Augustine, de C.[City of God?] 16:32.) and to David, 1 Paralipomenon 21:15., and 22:1.

3:2.text: Second. Hebrew adds, (Du Hamel) "in the second day of the second month."

3:3.text: By the first, or ancient measure, used before the captivity. The Babylonian cubit was a hand smaller, as Ezechiel (xl. 3.) intimates.
Solomon used the same cubit as Moses, but doubled the proportions.

3:4.text: A hundred. The Arabic and some copies of the Septuagint read only twenty. Capellus and Toinard would substitute, ten cubits. A small change in the Hebrew might occasion these variations.

In 3 Kings 6:2., we find the height was thirty cubits: but that might be only to the first floor. Many think that the temple was 120 cubits high: but Salien (the year of the world 3024) explains it of the four-square tower, (Menochius) in the front.

3:5.text: House, between the most holy place and the porch, (Tirinus) or it may comprise all but the porch. Hebrew, "he covered (or boarded) all this great house."
Deal boards interspersed with those of cedar, 3 Kings 6:9.
Fine, (obrizi.) Hebrew, "of Parvaim, which is probably the same as Sepharvaim, Ophir, etc., not far from Media and Colchis.

3:8.text: Amounting to about, is not in Hebrew.

3:9.text: Sicles. This weight seems enormous for each nail. Mariana supposes every nail weighed one sicle. But who will believe that only fifty were used? Hebrew, "And the weight of the nails was fifty," etc. The Syriac and Arabic omit this verse entirely.

3:10.text: Work. Hebrew, "of the work of young men," or resembling them.
Septuagint, "the work of wood." Vulgate literally, "of sculpture work," (Haydock) as Le Clerc renders the original. Some moderns translate, "of a work like men in motion."
The cherubims were in an erect posture.

3:11.text: Cubits, comprising the breadth of their body. Each occupied ten cubits.

3:13.text: Toward. Hebrew, "inward;" looking at each other, (Exodus 25:20.) or towards the altar of holocausts.

3:14.text: A veil. Abulensis places it between the court and the holy: but Jansenius would have it before the holy of holies. It seems rather that there was a veil in both places, (Barad. t. 2:b. 3:23.; Menochius) as Josephus ([Antiquities?] 8:2., and 12:10.) clearly intimates; and St. Paul speaks of the second veil, Hebrews 9:3. It is not certain which of them was torn at the death of Jesus Christ, Matthew 27:51. Cherubims; extraordinary figures.

3:15.text: Five. 3 Kings 7:15., we read each was eighteen cubits. It is probable that each was 17.5 and the other half was for the crown, (Calmet) or cornice.

3:16.text: Hundred, for one row, or 200 for both, 3 Kings.

3:17.text: Booz. "Stability and strength" are derived from God alone. Thus Solomon designed to insinuate that God established the harmony of the universe.
An orrery, or globe, was therefore placed on each of these pillars.

4:1.text: Altar of holocausts, much larger than that of Moses. The one which was built after the captivity, was a square of fifteen cubits. This of Solomon was only ten in height. A sloping ascent was made up to it, according to Josephus; (Jewish Wars 6:5., or 5:14.) or the steps were closed in, if we believe others. See Exodus 27:1., and 20:26. It is thought (Calmet) that the inside of the altar was filled with earth, (Menochius) or with rough stones: (Exodus 20:24.; Du Hamel) but that is not certain, with regard to the brazen altar. See 1 Machabees 4:44, 47.

4:2.text: Sea, or great brazen vessel, and some other things that were not in the tabernacle of Moses, whose plan was followed, but with greater magnificence.

4:3.text: Oxen. In 3 Kings 7:24., we read Hebrew, "knops," (Haydock) or "apples."
Of ten. Hebrew, "ten in a cubit." Hence there must have been 600 heads of oxen, as the sea was thirty cubits in circumference, and there were two rows.
Others suppose that there were only five in each row, or only one in each cubit. See Vatable and 3 Kings. The Septuagint and Syriac omit this verse.
Yet it is found in the best editions of the Septuagint.

4:4.text: Cast. Hebrew adds, "when it (the sea) was cast."
They were done at the same time, (Tirinus) and were perhaps intended to let out the water. The twelve oxen, which supported the sea, were not cast along with it.

4:5.text: Three. 3 Kings two, may specify the usual quantity that was contained, though the vessel would absolutely hold a thousand measures, or baths, more.

4:6.text: Holocaust, as well as all the necessary utensils.
Priests. They did not bathe in the sea, but drew water into other vessels. The Chaldean supposes that the work of Beseleel was for the high priest alone.

4:9.text: Hall, (basilicam.) Septuagint use the same term (aule) as just before is rendered court; and the Protestants make no distinction, though the Hebrew terms be different. Septuagint render chatsar, "the court" of the people. There, we know, that Solomon had his tribune, 2 Paralipomenon 6:13. Ezechiel (xliii. 14.) distinguishes two; the great, lower or outward, (Calmet chatsar) or court, and that of the priests, (Haydock) which he styles the less, etc. The principal door lay to the east.

4:10.text: Right, though to the left of those who entered the temple.

4:12.text: Pommels, (epistylia.) Hebrew, "cordons," or rows of pomegranates, (3 Kings 7:18.) each consisting of one hundred; so that as the two pillars were adorned with a double row, both together would have four hundred.
The precise number is fixed at ninety-six for each row, Jeremias 52:23.

4:16.text: His father. Hebrew Abib, which is considered by some as a surname, (Menochius) or the title may be given to Hiram, on account of his skill.
He is so called by the king of Tyre, (chap. 2:13.) for having instructed him in the true faith.

4:17.text: Saredatha, or Sarthan, (3 Kings 7:46.) not far from Bethsan, 3 Kings 4:12.

4:21.text: Flowers. They represented lilies or other flowers (Tirinus) full-blown, when the lamps were burning. All the branches were of pure gold.

4:22.text: Mortars, to hold the fine flour.
And thus. Here the fifth chapter commences, in Hebrew and Septuagint.

5:1.text: God, under the care of Selemith. See 1 Paralipomenon 26:26.

5:2.text: Year of the World 3001, Year before Christ 1003.
Ark. Another was not made, as that was most sacred and excellent.

5:4.text: Levites, with the priests alternately; (3 Kings 8:2.) or the latter were all Levites, (Calmet) though higher in dignity, in the same manner as our bishops are priests.

5:5.text: With the. "And" is omitted in Hebrew but supplied by Protestants, etc., from 3 Kings.

5:9.text: Oracle, pushing out the veil, (Haydock) in the tabernacle of Moses, but not in the temple, which was of larger dimensions.
But if. Hebrew, "but they were not seen without. And there it is till this day."

5:10.text: The other things which had been placed in, or at least on the side of the ark, were now removed to the treasury rooms.

5:11.text: Sanctified; free from all legal impediments to offer sacrifice.

Protestants, "and they did not then wait by course," which was the case also with the singers, etc., on this solemn occasion; as the number regulated by David for each week, would not have been sufficient.
The regulation had not yet been reduced to practice.
As yet (Du Hamel) at that time, is omitted (Haydock) by the Septuagint (Du Hamel) and Hebrew.

5:12.text: Linen, or "robes of silk." Protestants, "white linen."

5:14.text: It is called the glory of the Lord, because its appearance was luminous and awful, being a symbol of the divine presence.

6:1.text: The promise had been made to Moses, Exodus 16:10., and 40:32.

6:12.text: Hands. Such external gestures promote attention in prayer; and hence were much used by holy people, at all times.

6:13.text: Scaffold, (basim.) The Hebrew (kiyor) is used for "a shell, (concha.; Tirinus) or bowl," as the throne was probably of the same form, and was placed on a square pedestal, at the higher end of the court of the people, (Calmet) which is here styled the temple, (basilicae) or "hall," 2 Paralipomenon 4:9. Septuagint aules. Hebrew chatsar, which denotes that great court where (Haydock) the body of the people stood bare-headed, before the Lord, (Grotius, etc.; 2 Paralipomenon 20:5.) in the open air.
We are assured that the king himself never sat in his tribune.
Others reserve that privilege to him alone.
While he addressed the people, he turned towards them.

6:19.text: End. Temples are erected for the convenience of men.
Solomon hopes that God will reward his good intentions.

6:22.text: Altar; thus solemnly consenting to be punished by God, if he swear falsely.
This practice was not prescribed by the law, but took place when witnesses could not be procured, or when it was necessary to rely on the word of the accused.

6:28.text: Blasting, or mildew, (aerugo et aurugo.) The first has the colour of brass, the other of gold. Both have nearly the same import.

One of the terms is rendered, corrupt air; (3 Kings 8:37.; Calmet) as this is one of the great causes why both plants and animals do not thrive. Septuagint, "corruption of the air and king's evil, or yellow jaundice:" ikteros.

6:29.text: Any. Hebrew adds, "man, or thy people." Septuagint, "and (that is) to thy." See ver. 32.
Both strangers and Israelites may there sue for mercy, and obtain it.

From this chapter, as well as from many other passages, it is evident that places consecrated to God, are more acceptable to him than other places; (Worthington) and here the prayers of the faithful are sooner heard, as they testify their unity.

6:31.text: Fear thee, being filled with awe at the sight of thy judgments.

6:33.text: House, that they may know that this is not only called, but is, in effect, the temple of the Lord, where he will display his power and goodness.

6:38.text: Name. Hence arose the custom of turning towards Jerusalem and the temple, when the Jews poured forth their supplications, in any country, 3 Kings 8:44.

6:41.text: Now, therefore. This is taken from Psalm 131:8, 9, 10., (Menochius) almost word for word. We do not find that Solomon spoke this, 3 Kings.
Come, O Lord, and dwell here.

Strength, by which thy wonders shine forth, and thy enemies are dismayed. The ark is styled the strength of Israel, Psalm 77:61., and 62:3.; and in the parallel passage we read, Thou and the ark of thy sanctification, or sanctuary, which is its highest ornament, and which thou hast sanctified, or required to be treated with respect.
Salvation, or justice, (Psalm cxxxi.) which is the surest method of obtaining happiness.

6:42.text: Anointed. Cover not with shame the king, whom thou hast appointed, (Haydock) nor reject my prayer.

Mercies, which thou hast shewn, or rather (Calmet) the acts of virtue which thy servant displayed. (Syriac, etc.) For thy servant David's sake, turn not away the face of thy anointed, Psalm cxxxi. In consideration of his merits, grant my request.
Even Solomon, though at this time the object of God's complacency, disdains not to screen himself under the merits of the departed saints.

7:2.text: Majesty; the cloud. For some time the priests were obliged to keep at a distance. They afterwards offered up many victims.

7:3.text: Glory. The same word was before rendered majesty.

7:4.text: Victims, by the hands of the priests: or they slew them themselves, and presented the blood and fat to be placed on the altar by the priests.

7:5.text: People, each in their proper sphere. The priests had the chief part, as the ministers of religion. The solemnity continued for a week, and many altars were erected in the court.

7:6.text: Which music, or Psalms, (carminum) David had composed.
Because. This was the chorus of the 135th Psalm, which was sung on this occasion by some, while others played on their musical instruments.
Literally, "singing by their hands." Protestants, "when David praised by their ministry."
As the temple was chosen by God for the place of prayer, so special forms of blessing, etc., are more agreeable to him.

7:9.text: Solemnity of tabernacles, (Menochius) which ensued after the dedication was ended.

7:10.text: Day. He had given them leave to depart, the day before, 3 Kings 8:66.

7:14.text: Face, to appease me, that I may look down upon them in mercy.

8:1.text: Year of the World 3012, Year before Christ 992.

8:2.text: Given back (Tirinus) as he refused to accept of the present. Some think that (Calmet) Hiram gave these cities in exchange for the others.

8:3.text: Emath, or Emesa, on the Orontes. This city had belonged to Thou, who cultivated the friendship of David, 2 Kings 8:9. But after the death of these princes, it had given some offence to Solomon, who took it, as well as Palmira, or Thadmor, though not perhaps in person.

8:4.text: Strong. Hebrew, "the cities of tents," Exodus 1:11.

8:8.text: Tributaries. Hebrew also, "to work," at cutting stones, etc., 1 Paralipomenon 22:2. Some of their descendants returned from captivity, 1 Paralipomenon 9:2.

8:10.text: Fifty. These were different from the 550 overseers, 3 Kings 9:23.

8:11.text: Into it. It was deemed improper for a pagan woman to reside in the same place.
She had perhaps pretended, at first, to be converted, but had lately given some signs of relapse.
Solomon was as yet far from communicating with infidels.

8:14.text: And gate, at their respective posts. See 1 Paralipomenon xxvi.

8:15.text: Treasures. The regulations of David were exactly observed.

8:17.text: Asiongaber, which was called Bernice, (Josephus, [Antiquities?] 8:2.) and now Suez.

8:18.text: Ships; not perhaps from Tyre, but from the Red Sea.
Others think that he sent them by a canal, which opened a communication between the Mediterranean and Suez, (Huet) the distance of about ninety miles.

But this canal seems to have been made after the age of Solomon. Hence others imagine that the ships were taken in pieces, or conveyed by land, as has been sometimes done. Mahomet II transported ships across the isthmus of Corinth. Alexander the Great conveyed on chariots the ships which had been used to cross the Indus, as far as the Hydaspes.
Skilful mariners. They were the most expert, and the inventers of navigation. Prima ratem ventis tradere docta Tyrus. (Tibul.) See Wisdom 14:6.
Ophir, the kingdom of Pegu, in the East Indies, (Tirinus) or some other distant land.
Fifty. Thirty are omitted, 3 Kings 9:28., as they were expended in the voyage.
Yet the Hebrew letters for twenty and fifty are extremely similar.

9:1.text: Saba. See 3 Kings 10:1. This queen resided in Arabia or Abyssinia.
Questions. They were not of a trifling nature, as she is commended by Christ.

9:3.text: House; the royal palace, or rather the temple, which was much more magnificent than any thing in the East.

9:5.text: Virtues. Hebrew, "words," or of what regards thee (Calmet) and thy exploits.

9:6.text: Fame. Hebrew and Septuagint, "the fame which I had heard."

9:8.text: King of the Lord. Hence the style, "By the grace of God, king of England," etc., has been adopted. Those deserve the name, who rule according to God's will.
The kings of the Jews were more properly lieutenants of God, as the government was a theocracy, 2 Paralipomenon 13:8., etc.

9:10.text: Hebrew algumim: 3 Kings almugim, as the letters are frequently transposed in Hebrew.
Huet thinks these were citron-trees, which did not produce fruit, but were used to make costly tables, and were a species of cedar.

9:11.text: Stairs. Hebrew, "hands," (Du Hamel) or banisters, from the palace to the temple.

9:12.text: And many. Hebrew, "besides that which she had brought unto the king." (Protestants) It is however certain that Solomon made use of many of her presents, and therefore he returned her others in greater profusion, or, as it is expressed, 3 Kings, besides what he offered her of himself of his royal bounty.

9:13.text: Gold, each worth 5475l. (Arbuthnot) so that this part of the revenue alone would amount to 3,646,350l. sterling, annually.

9:14.text: Beside. Hebrew, "besides that which chapmen and merchants brought." (Protestants) The deputies appear to be the tax-gatherers, 3 Kings 10:15.

9:16.text: Pieces. In 3 Kings, we read, three pounds.
Wood; or, "in the palace of the forest of Libanus," (3 Kings 10:17.; Calmet) as it is expressed in the Septuagint.

9:18.text: Hebrew in 3 Kings seems clearer and more correct.

9:20.text: Days. We sometimes find the figure, hyperbole, used in Scripture for things unusual, 2 Paralipomenon 1:16.

9:21.text: Tharsis. Any distant place was so called.

9:25.text: Thousand. In 3 Kings, we read, 1400 chariots; and here Hebrew has, "4000 stables, (Calmet) or stalls for horses and chariots;" (Protestants) and the Septuagint, "4000 mares for the chariots, and 12,000 horsemen."
There might be ten horses in each stable.

9:29.text: Seer. The works of these three are not extant.
The fall of Solomon is recorded, 3 Kings xi.

9:31.text: Year of the World 3029, Year before Christ 975.

10:7.text: Please. 3 Kings xii., "serve." By a little condescension, thou mayst establish thy throne.
The people did not complain, till they saw the riches of the nation wasted on wicked women (Tirinus) and idols.

10:11.text: Scorpions; thorns, or scourges armed with hooks.
Roboam had not sense to despise the foolish advice of the young men.

10:15.text: Will of God: not that the king should act foolishly, or the people rebel, but to effect what he had promised.

Syriac has well expressed the sense: "because this rebellion happened by the divine permission, that they might behold the truth of the prediction of Ahias;" (Calmet) and that the fault of Solomon might be punished.

10:16.text: People. Hebrew, "and when all Israel saw that the king would not hearken unto them, the people answered the king, saying: What portion have we in David?"
Dwellings. Many ancient Latin manuscripts here insert 3 Kings 12:20.

11:1.text: Year of the World 3030, Year before Christ 974.
Chosen. Septuagint, "young," (Haydock) as the Hebrew term also signifies.
His. Hebrew, "the kingdom again to Roboam."

11:4.text: Will, to punish the disorders of Solomon, etc. See 3 Kings 12:24., and Genesis 45:8. God knows how to draw good out of evil, without approving the misconduct of men.

11:5.text: Juda. This was now become a frontier country, and both kings were forced to erect fortifications, 3 Kings 12:25.

11:6.text: Etam, famous for fine waters and gardens, (Josephus, [Antiquities?] 8:2.) about ten miles from Jerusalem, on the road to Hebron. It was fortified against the inroads of Simeon and Dan.

11:11.text: Governors, of a military character. Hebrew, "captains, (Tirinus) and store of provisions, (wheat) and of oil," etc., such as would be requisite in a siege.

11:14.text: Off. This was done at different times; so that almost all the Levites took refuge in the dominions of Juda. Yet some prevaricated. Ezechiel (xliv. 12.) only exempts the family of Sadoc.
The rest set before us a noble example to endure temporal loss, rather than to neglect the practice of religion, or to conform to a false one.

11:15.text: Septuagint express this by two names, "for the idols, and for the fools."
The Mendesians of Egypt, solemnly worshipped the goat; and Pan was represented nearly in the same form. Yet it is probable that the Hebrews style the idols by this name out of derision, 3 Kings 15:2.

11:17.text: Only is not expressed in Hebrew or Septuagint, but it is sufficiently implied.

11:18.text: Daughter. Hebrew ban, "the son." But bath, "daughter," (Haydock) occurs properly in some manuscripts, as in the ancient versions. Leusden has here this very odd note: "perhaps by ban, (with a pathah) here both sexes may be understood." Forte per ban hic intelligitur uterque sexus.

11:20.text: Daughter, or grand-daughter, by Thamar, 3 Kings 15:2.

11:22.text: Grotius maintains that Abia was the eldest. But all others are of a contrary opinion, and believe that Roboam derogated from the law, (Deuteronomy 21:16.; Calmet) as David had done, unless it was left to the option of the king to appoint whom he pleased for his successor.
Meant. Protestants supply, "he thought" which is found in the Septuagint.

11:23.text: Because. Septuagint, "And he was magnified above....and he gave them (cities, autais) abundance," etc. This may be all understood of Abia. But Hebrew rather speaks of his father: "And he acted wisely, and dispersed all his other children, throughout the countries of Juda;" (Haydock) keeping them at a distance, to prevent any commotion, (Calmet) but making ample provision and procuring wives for them, perhaps at no small cost, as few might wish to be thus prisoners at large.
It was probably then the custom to educate the royal children in the noblest families. See 4 Kings 10:1.
This would also tend to increase their father's influence, and to make them better acquainted with the country, that they might be able to communicate all necessary intelligence.
Pagnin and Vatable insinuate, that Roboam "caused to be educated and dispersed some of all his children, throughout all the lands of Juda."

12:1.text: Israel, in his dominions. The kingdom of Jeroboam had long before apostatized, though there were still many true servants of God, who would not bend the knee before Baal, ver. 12.

12:2.text: Year of the World 3033, Year before Christ 971.

12:3.text: Troglodites, who dwell in caverns, near the Red Sea. Pliny ([Natural History?] 5:8., and 6:29.) speaks of the city Suca, which in Hebrew means a tent, as here we read succiyim, (Haydock) "dwelling in tents;" which some explain of the Arabs, who are called Scenites, on the same account.
People of this description, without any fixed abode, inhabited the Stony as well as the Desert Arabia, 2 Paralipomenon 14:14.

12:7.text: A little help. I will not suffer them to be quite destroyed. Hebrew also signifies, "shortly."
Fall, (stillabit) like drops of water, even to the last.
Even when he punishes, he does not let the whole flood of his indignation fall upon the guilty.

12:10.text: Bearers. Hebrew, "runners," as the guards attended the king on foot.

12:12.text: For even. Protestants, "and also in Judah things went well;" (Haydock) or, "he spoke good words even to Juda."
Septuagint, "for even in Juda there were good words, and in all pleasing." Words are often put for works.
The Lord regarded the fidelity of some, and the humility of the princes.

This advantage was derived from tribulation, that the king entered into himself, at least for a time: (Haydock) but he was not constant, ver. 14. After the scourge was withdrawn, he presently relapsed. He had betrayed great cowardice, as even his son confessed; and had probably been treated with great indignity, (Tirinus) as it was the custom with Sesac, or Sesostris, to chain the conquered princes to his triumphal car, and to leave obscene representations of women in the country.
Sesostris proceeded to the conquest of Asia.

12:14.text: Lord, with sincerity, being actuated only by a servile fear. He died impenitent.

12:15.text: And diligently. Hebrew, "in their genealogical accounts." (Haydock) See 2 Paralipomenon 13:22.

13:1.text: Year of the World 3046, Year before Christ 958.; 3 Kings xv.

13:2.text: Michaia, alias Maacha. Her father had also two names; viz., Absalom or Abessalom, and Uriel.
Of Gabaa, may refer to the women.

13:3.text: War. The author of the new edition of St. Jerome mentions two manuscripts and there are several printed editions, which reduce these numbers to one half. Castalio adopts this correction, asserting that the Greek and Josephus are on his side, which is not true.

We must however observe, that there is some doubt with respect to Josephus, (Haydock) as some of the oldest Latin editions acknowledge the smaller numbers; and Vignoles (I. 29.) conjectures, that the Greek copies did the same formerly, since Aborbanel "accuses Josephus of having made Jeroboam's loss no more than 50,000, contrary to the Hebrew text," ver. 17. This learned author is of opinion, that the Jews sometimes expressed their numbers by figures, (Kennicott) analogous to those which the later Arabs borrowed from the Indians; though, in more ancient times, the letters of the alphabet had been used. Both customs seem to have prevailed; (Erpenius) and this may account for many variations, as the omission of a single cypher, resembling our period (.), might easily occasion much confusion. Kennicott (Diss. i., and ii.) seems to suspect, that the ancient versions have been made to agree with the corrupted Hebrew, no less than Josephus; and that many of the large numbers, recorded in Scripture, (which might countenance what we here read) are incorrect. See 2 Paralipomenon 17:13, 19., and 14:8., comp.[compare] with 16:1., and 25:6., comp.[compare] with 4 Kings 13:7. It is certain (Haydock) many Latin manuscripts confirm the edition of Sixtus V, which has the smaller numbers; and this is the case with most of the older manuscripts and editions. How St. Jerome expressed it, we cannot easily determine. The editor of his works has been guided chiefly by the Memmian canon. See 2 Kings 15:7. Lyranus quotes St. Jerome, as reading 50,000; (ver. 7) and it is so expressed in the Paris editions of 1534 and (Haydock) 1546, of the Hebrew questions on Paralipomenon. But Erasmus doubts whether this be the genuine work of St. Jerome, (Kennicott) or in the state in which he left it. If we consult the more ancient manuscripts and editions, prior to that of Clement VIII, we shall allow that they are more favourable to the smaller numbers, on this occasion; and are more likely to exhibit the pure version of the holy doctor, (Haydock) as the later editions have had "the Hebrew fountains" also in view.

If however we adhere to the received numbers, (Haydock) we must remember that kings, at that time, led forth to battle all their subjects who were able to bear arms. Under Moses there were 600,000. David found 1,100,000 in Israel, and 470,000 in Juda, exclusive of Levi and Benjamin; (chap. 21:5.) or, at least, as it is expressed [in] 2 Kings 24:9., in all, 1,300,000. Asa was at the head of 580,000, 2 Paralipomenon 14:8., etc.

13:4.text: Semeron, on which Samaria was afterwards built. Hebrew reads, "Mount Tsemaraim." There was a city of this name in Benjamin.
But this was a mount. Hebrew, "in Mount Ephraim."

13:5.text: A covenant of salt. That is, a firm and perpetual covenant. See Numbers 18:19.

It seemed this covenant was to be inviolable; and it had its effect, notwithstanding the crimes of many of the kings. The Arabs still regard the partaking of bread and salt as the most sacred covenant.
"The ancients prayed when they placed salt before their guests, that, as salt partook of two natures, so they might be cemented together in concord."

Abia speaks with much prudence, and with an air of piety, though his heart was corrupt, 3 Kings 15:3. That of his adversary was still more so; and God granted victory to the former, to reward even the appearance of virtue in the king, and the sincere piety of some of his followers.

13:7.text: Vain. The same term is used as in Matthew 5:22.
But Roboam was not very young; (3 Kings 14:21.) and we may adhere to the Vulgate.
Septuagint, "he was a youth, and timid."
Abia speaks of his father with little respect, and seems to have forgotten that he had laid down his arms in obedience to God's order.

13:8.text: And golden. These idols will enervate and render your great numbers useless.
We have a just cause and religion is on our side.

13:9.text: Rams. These were sacrificed by the false priests, who acted in a very different manner from those of the Lord, Leviticus viii., and ix.

13:11.text: Table. It is hence inferred, that Sesac had only left one: (Lyranus; Torniel.) but this does not follow.

13:14.text: Trumpets, to invoke God's aid, Numbers 10:9.

13:15.text: Terrified. Septuagint, "struck;" God caused the victory to declare in favour of those who were only half the number.

13:17.text: Five. Some Latin editions have 50,000, (Calmet) which number is found in some manuscripts of Epiphanius.
Hentenius has the same, and only remarks five manuscripts which had the greater number. See ver. 3.
We do not read of such a slaughter in any one battle. But Israel made no resistance, and God assisted the four hundred thousand.
So many were either slain or wounded.

13:19.text: Bethel, the head of the calf worship; though Abulensis thinks it was another city, otherwise it would not have been spared. The calf continued there till the reign of Jehu, 4 Kings 10:29.
Jesana; perhaps Senna, (Numbers 34:4.; Calmet) seven miles north of Jericho.
Josue (xv. 9.) places Mount Ephron between Juda and Ephraim; and we find a town of the name, (1 Machabees 5:46.) but on the other side of the Jordan.

13:20.text: Him. Some think Abia, as Jeroboam survived him two years: but others, (Calmet) with greater probability, (Du Hamel) suppose that the latter is here specified, and that he fell a victim to the divine wrath, as the prophet Ahias had foretold.

13:22.text: Works. Protestants, "sayings are written in the story of the prophets Iddo."
Modross properly means "researches," and was the title of the genealogical work of this prophet, from which this account is probably taken, 2 Paralipomenon 12:15.
The entire work is not extant.

14:1.text: Year of the World 3049, Year before Christ 955.; 3 Kings xv.
Year of the World 3063, Year before Christ 941.
Years, dating from the fifth; as in the fifteenth year of his reign, Asa offered many victims, which had been taken from the enemy, 2 Paralipomenon 15:10.

14:2.text: God, with respect to the external worship of the true God, (Haydock) though he had some faults, 3 Kings 15:14.

Places, while he tolerated those which were intended for the service of the Lord, (Calmet) though illegally; and he even left the buildings erected by Solomon standing, (4 Kings 23:13.; Tirinus) thinking they might be no longer dangerous.
Several other pious kings did the like, for which they deserved some reprehension; as they could not be ignorant, that this was considered as wrong by the more enlightened, Ecclesiasticus 49:5.
It was the king's duty to destroy the exterior practices of idolatry, while the priests and prophets were to instruct.

14:5.text: Altars. Hebrew, "high places, and the Chamanim," or temples of the sun, Leviticus 26:30.
Asa was encouraged to perfect the work which he had begun, by the exhortation of Azarias, 2 Paralipomenon 15:8.

14:6.text: Admirable lesson for all to prepare for war in time of quiet!

14:9.text: Ethiopian. Some think Zara was a successor of Sesac; and the Rabbins say, that he "restored the spoils which the latter had taken."
He at least, made the Jews some compensation for that loss. Perhaps he had been present in the expedition of Sesac, and was now animated with the same ambition.
It would be difficult for a foreign king to traverse Egypt with so large an army, (Calmet) unless he had conquered or intimidated the king of the country.
Hence some believe that the Ethiopia here in question, was a part of Arabia.

14:9.src: Salien, the year of the world 3094.; Tirinus
14:9.text: But it is more probable that it lay south of Egypt, as the troops of Zara are joined with the Lybians, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8.
A king of Arabia would hardly have had so many subjects.
Three. Some would read thirteen, as Anani takes notice of the great number of chariots, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8.
But surely the number here specified is sufficient. The minds of men are very different. In the former chapter we find them complaining of the too great numbers!

14:10.text: Meet him, fearing lest he should lay waste his dominions, as Sesac had done.
Sephata, or Sephala, a great valley to the west of Maresa, (Calmet) which is a city of Juda.
Septuagint have, "in the vale north of Maresa."

14:11.text: Help. Hebrew, "to help; whether with many, or with those who have no power." The same sentiment was expressed by Jonathas, 1 Kings 14:6. Those who have God with them are secure of victory.
If God be with us, who is against us?

14:12.text: Terrified. Hebrew and Septuagint, "struck," as he had done Jeroboam, 2 Paralipomenon 13:15.

14:13.text: Gerara was on the southern borders, twenty-three miles from Maresa.
The Ethiopians were pursued perhaps for many days.
Destruction. Protestants, "that they could not recover themselves." Hebrew, "there was no quarter given;" or, "so that none were left alive."
If this were the case, the number of the slain far exceeded that the Jeroboam's army, 2 Paralipomenon 13.

14:14.text: Gerara. The adjacent territory is now uncultivated, Josue 13:3.
Great. Hebrew, "a fear of the Lord;" (ver. 12) an extraordinary (Calmet) panic.
The Philistines had assisted the enemy, and therefore their cities were invaded; (Menochius) or these belonged to Zara.

14:15.text: Destroyed. Hebrew, "smote also the tents of cattle." Septuagint, "they cut in pieces the tents of cattle, the Amazons;" or, according to the Vatican copy, (Haydock) "the Alimazonians," as they seem to have read aeli manim, which may be well rendered, "the tents of the Mineans," a people of Arabia, 2 Paralipomenon 20:1., and 26:7., and Judges 10:11.

If these nations came to punish Asa, for banishing their foreign worship, (ver. 12) as the Sieur de Folard thinks the sacred penman insinuates, (ver. 5) we need not wonder that they met with such ill success, and that not one of them was spared, as God avenged his own cause, ver. 12, 13.

We read of no cavalry here, though it is mentioned, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8. It seems not to have been considerable, as the people of Asia, the Greeks and Romans, trusted more to their infantry; and, in effect, no species of warfare is more costly to a state, nor less useful, than a multitude of horses, which were introduced in barbarous times.

15:1.text: Azarias is called Oded in Hebrew, ver. 8.
There was a high priest of the name of Azarias, about the same time.

15:2.text: With him. He will continue thus to protect you, if you prove faithful. The Pelagians hence inferred, that man was to prepare his own heart for the reception of grace. But the text only speaks of external aid; and though it were understood of internal grace, (Calmet) we may merit an increase of grace, by a faithful co-operation (Worthington) with that with which God has presented us.

15:3.text: Shall pass, is not found in Hebrew, etc., and many supply, "had been."
The Jews, and some interpreters, would explain all this of what had passed already; (Calmet) and this is the idea of the Protestants, "Now, for a long season, Israel hath been without," etc.

But the conclusion convinces us that this is a prediction; (ver. 7) take courage. You shall not be involved in these miseries. Some explain the whole of the kingdom of Israel, which, from its commencement till after its final ruin, persevered in its rebellion against the true God.
Others think the kingdom of Juda was also concerned, and its state of captivity at Babylon foretold.

But its full completion includes the latter ages, particularly after the murder of the Messias, (Tirinus) and seems of the same nature as the prophecy of Osee 3:4., as our Saviour himself insinuates, Matthew 24:6, 9, 13.

15:4.text: And when. Septuagint, "and he shall convert them to the Lord,...and shall be found by them."
This will be verified in the last days, Romans 11:26.

15:5.text: Cometh in, in public or private transactions. See Leviticus 26:36.

15:6.text: Nation, both in the times of Baasa, who fought against Asa, and afterwards. For the same prophecy may be literally explained of more things than one.
Distress. Our Saviour says, Then shall they deliver you up to be afflicted, etc. But he that shall persevere to the end, he shall be saved. [Matthew 24:9-13.]

15:8.text: Azarias, the son, is omitted in Hebrew and the Vatican Septuagint, which reads Adad, (Haydock) but it is supplied in the other editions, and in the Syriac, (Calmet) as [in] ver. 1.
Taken: we do not read on what occasion, 2 Paralipomenon 17:2. His father had seized upon Bethel, etc., 2 Paralipomenon 13:19.
Yet Asa waged war himself with the king of Israel.

Dedicated. Septuagint, "renewed," as the altar had been neglected, or injured by the continual fire; (Menochius) or this was the extraordinary (Haydock) altar, which Solomon had used at the dedication of the temple. The multitude of Asa's victims required more than one, ver. 11. It is not probable that God's worship had been neglected till the 15th year of this pious king, (ver. 9) since even his wicked father was punctual in this respect, 2 Paralipomenon 13:11. Before, at the higher end of the priests' court, to the east of the altar of holocausts.

15:9.text: Simeon. This tribe was intimately connected with that of Juda, though many had revolted. Some afterwards returned, as they did from other parts, actuated either by motives of interest or of religion, 2 Paralipomenon 34:6., and 3 Kings 11:13.

15:10.text: Month, Sivan, or May, when the feast of Pentecost occurs.

15:11.text: Spoils, which they had sold, and now testified their gratitude.

15:12.text: He went. Hebrew and Septuagint, "They proceeded, according to the covenant, to seek."
They swore upon the altar, or passed between the divided victims, Genesis 15:9, 17.
This solemn act was usual, and more necessary in times of trouble.

15:13.text: Said he, is not in Hebrew, etc.

15:15.text: Septuagint, "all Juda rejoiced on account of the oath."

15:16.text: There were perhaps two of the name, and Abia had married one as well as Roboam.
Deposed, etc. Hebrew, "from being queen." Septuagint, "he removed Maacha,...that she should not minister to Astarte; and he cut the idol in pieces, and burnt it," etc.
Syriac, "because she had celebrated a feast to idols." But it seems she had set up a statue, which Asa burnt. See 3 Kings 15:13.
Priapus is added by the Vulgate to explain [the] Hebrew, "the idol of, or in, the grove," (Haydock) Asera or Astarte.

15:17.text: Israel, to the honour only of the true God, 2 Paralipomenon 14:2., and 3 Kings 15:14.
Perfect, with respect to the external worship,
Other altars, besides that at Jerusalem, might be dispensed with, (Worthington) by God's authority.

15:18.text: Vowed, in the wars against Jeroboam and Zara. See 1 Paralipomenon 26:27.

15:19.src: Calmet; Torniel, the year of the world 3094.
15:19.text: Year. Usher dates from the separation of the two kingdoms; and this year corresponds with the 15th of Asa, ver. 10. See 3 Kings 15:16.

16:1.text: Year of the World 3064, Year before Christ 940.
Six and thirtieth year of his kingdom. That is, of the kingdom of Juda, taking the date of it from the beginning of the reign of Roboam.
It was the 16th of Asa. We read that Baasa died in the 26th year of Asa, 3 Kings 16:8. How then could he fight with him in the 36th?

Rama was on an eminence, and commanded the pass below. Baasa wished to cut off all communication with the kingdom of Juda, as he knew many of his subjects had emigrated for the sake of the true religion, 2 Paralipomenon 15:9.
He had taken the city from the tribe of Benjamin.

16:3.text: There is, Hebrew is indeterminate: "a league," etc. Septuagint, "Make a league....behold I have sent thee gold and silver. Come and drive away from me Baasa, king," etc.
Asa induces the king of Damascus to act perfidiously.
Otherwise it is not unlawful to make use of the arms of infidels, unless where God has forbidden it.
David had recourse to Achis, and the Machabees to the Romans.

Some kings are blamed for making leagues with the princes of Israel, because they had been warned to the contrary; and Asa was severely reprehended, as he had already received such assurances of the divine protection, (chap. 14:12., and 15:7.) that nothing but pusillanimity could have induced him (Haydock) to give away the sacred treasures, in order to obtain this aid of the Syrian king, ver. 7.

16:4.text: Nephthali. This seem preferable to the Hebrew reading, 3 Kings 15:20.
Protestants, "all the store-cities." Septuagint, "all the environs."
Arabic, "all the arsenals of the cities of Nephthali."

16:7.text: Syria. It seems more natural to read Israel.

But we must remember that Benadad was an ally of Israel; and if he had not been bribed, he would have come to the assistance of Baasa, (Haydock) and thus both might have fallen a prey to Asa, as the much greater forces of Zara had done.

16:9.text: Behold. Protestants, "run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to shew himself strong in the behalf of them whose heart is perfect towards him." Septuagint, "to shew power in every heart full, or perfect, in his regard."
Asa fell on this occasion through human frailty, but rose again by repentance.
Thee, as they were till the death of Baasa, 3 Kings 15:32.

16:10.text: Prison. Literally, "in bonds," (nervum) made of leather thongs or nerves, (Haydock) or of iron, to confine either the neck or the feet.
Hebrew, "the house of disturbance." Septuagint, etc., "prison." Some explain it (Calmet) of the stocks to enclose the neck.
Time, either because they expressed the same sentiments as the prophet, (Calmet) or because they disapproved of his imprisonment.
Septuagint, "Asa made havoc among the people," etc.

16:12.text: Year of the World 3087, Year before Christ 917.
Most, etc. Hebrew, "till his disease got upwards," (Calmet) to the head (Tirinus) and heart, (Haydock) when the gout generally proves fatal.

Septuagint, "till he was very ill:" (Haydock) a just punishment for his having confined the prophet in fetters; but of a temporal nature, as he sinned through passion, and died penitent, his heart being perfect (chap. 15:17.) all or the most part of his days, particularly in the last.

Yet it was not the having recourse to them, with some degree of confidence, that is here reprehended, but the placing too much trust in men, (Calmet) and too little in God, the sovereign arbiter of life and death.

16:14.text: Sepulchre. Hebrew, "sepulchres," as there were many separate apartments in the same cavern.
Asa had prepared one cell, as David and Solomon had done.
Odoriferous (mertriciis.) Such as harlots delight in, (Proverbs 7:16,) to entice the sensual.
Hebrew zenim, may be derived from zana, fornicari. It denotes a mixture of perfumes.
But here the Vulgate read zunim.
Hebrew and Septuagint, "they laid him on a bed, and filled it with aromatical spices, and with various sorts of perfumers' ointments, and they made him a very great funeral, or (Haydock) burning."

It is not clear whether the body was placed on a bed of state, and these perfumes were used to remove every disagreeable smell, or the body itself was rather consumed along with them, a practice which seems to have become more common since the days of Asa, Jeremias 34:5., 1 Kings 31:12., and Amos 6:10. Joram was deprived of this honour, 2 Paralipomenon 21:19.

Sanctius adduces many examples, to prove that the spices were burnt only near the body; (Tirinus) and the Hebrews generally preferred to inter the corpse. Corpora condere quam cremare è more Aegyptio.

17:1.text: Year of the World 3090, Year before Christ 914.
Israel. In consequence of the civil broils, which ensued after the death of Baasa. The two kingdoms had been nearly equal.

17:2.text: Numbers, who were kept on foot; probably as in the days of David.

17:3.text: First. Before his fall: some copies add, "and last," (La Haye) as the Gothic manuscript used by Mariana does, agreeably to the Scripture style.

17:5.text: Presents, as was customary, (1 Kings 10:27,) or tribute.

17:6.text: When. Hebrew, "And his heart was elevated in the ways of the Lord." He was endued with courage to undertake the great work of banishing all abuses out of his dominions, seeing that the people were well affected, and the Lord urged him forward.

He trusted no so much to his great army or riches, as to a good conscience, (Tirinus) which made him fear no difficulties; (1 John 3:21,) doubting not but God would grant him success, as he endeavoured to keep his laws.
Groves of idols, (Menochius) though he tolerated the high places, which had been consecrated to God, 2 Paralipomenon 20:33., and 3 Kings 22:43.
Good works, rewarded by God, encouraged people to proceed in virtue.

17:7.text: Year of the World 3092, Year before Christ 912.
Princes. Political, (Tirinus) to see that all paid attention to the proper teachers; (Malachias 2:7.; Calmet) and thus to partake in that good work.
The princes might also give instruction by word (Calmet) and example.

17:9.text: Book. The Pentateuch, or its abridgment, the book of Deuteronomy, (Menochius) which they read and explained.
People. It was not sufficient to destroy idolatry, (Menochius) unless the people were taught how to serve the true God.

17:10.text: War. They had witnessed the fruitless attempt of Zara, 2 Paralipomenon 14:15.

17:11.text: Philistines. Though they had been reduced by David, they had sometimes proved remiss in the payment of tribute.

Arabians, who lived near the Ethiopians, and the Dead Sea, 2 Paralipomenon 21:19. They had been subdued by Solomon, (3 Kings 10:15.) and were rich in cattle, Ezechiel 27:21. The name is not so comprehensive as we generally make it.

17:12.text: Houses. Protestants, "castles." Hebrew biraniyoth. Birah is properly a Chaldean word, and is added after susan, to imply that it was a royal castle, (Calmet) or palace.
Septuagint often use baris in the same sense.
It became a provincial word in Palestine, for a place shut up on all sides and built like a tower.
David calls the temple a palace (habbirah) for God, 1 Paralipomenon 29:1.
Josaphat employed his immense riches in beautifying the country, and erecting storehouses, ver. 5.
Walled. Protestants, "cities of store." (Haydock) See 2 Paralipomenon 15:4.

17:14.text: Ednas was the chief of the five generals, who had under their command 1,160,000, (Calmet) besides the garrison soldiers, ver. 19.
So great power and riches had not been seen in Juda since the days of Solomon.
They were not all on duty at the same time, but were able to bear arms (Menochius) when called out.

17:16.text: Consecrated. Hebrew, "a volunteer of the Lord," (Haydock) serving at his own expence, (Tirinus) or with remarkable zeal.
Perhaps he was of the tribe of Levi, (Menochius) or had taken the vows of the Nazarites, etc.

17:17.text: After. Hebrew and Septuagint, "And of Benjamin, Eliada, a valiant man of the army."
It seems all were not chosen from Juda.

17:19.text: King, at different times. Septuagint, "waiting upon" him, (Haydock) like the companies which David had appointed, (Calmet) but far more numerous.

The dominions of Juda were now pretty extensive, and well cultivated. The population must have amounted to near six millions, for whose support recourse must have been had to commerce. Rome had once almost four million inhabitants, and Cairo is said to contain seven millions, while China has two hundred millions.
We must reflect that many from the other tribes had taken refuge in the kingdom of Juda.
It would be too bold to suppose, with Kennicott, that so many numbers have been corrupted.

18:1.text: Affinity, taking Achab's daughter for his son's wife.

18:2.text: Year of the World 3107, Year before Christ 897.

18:3.text: War. Josaphat was too complaisant. He was rebuked by the son of Hanani, as Asa had been by his father; (chap. 16:7.) and, not long after, Eliezer reproached him for joining in alliance with Ochozias, 2 Paralipomenon 20.
He acted without an ill intention, and was more easily pardoned.

18:14.text: Hands. He ironically (Calmet) alludes to the declarations of the false prophets.

18:18.text: Ye. In 3 Kings, he addresses his discourse chiefly to Achab.

18:20.text: Said. See 3 Kings xxii. The Rabbins pretend that the spirit of Naboth came forth. But it was satan, or an evil spirit.

18:21.text: So. The Lord permits evil, but he does not approve of it.
Homer makes Jupiter send a deluding dream to Agamemnon.
The delusive predictions of impostors, are one great source of calamities.

18:25.text: Amelech. Septuagint and Syriac, "to Joas, the commander, son of the father.
He had perhaps the government of affairs at Samaria, during his king." Achab's absence.

18:27.text: People. Mark what I say. The event will quickly prove whether I am a prophet or an impostor.

18:29.text: Dress. Protestants, "disguised himself, and they went." See 3 Kings 22:30.

18:31.text: Turned. Hebrew, "moved them from him." They felt that he was under God's protection; and discovered that he was not the king of Israel.

19:2.text: Thou. Septuagint, "O king Josaphat, if thou helpest the sinner; or, wilt thou befriend one hated by the Lord?" Protestants, "shouldst thou....love them that hate the Lord?"

The Donatists hence inferred, that it was not lawful to converse with sinners. But St. Augustine (contra ep. Par. 2:18.) replied, that Josaphat was blamed for aiding Achab in the contempt of the true prophet, and for going to battle conformably to the predictions of impostors. Both kings offended, but in a different degree.
We must also keep at as great a distance as possible from the notorious enemies of God, as their conversation is dangerous.
Evil communications corrupt the best of manners. See Psalm cxxxviii., etc.

19:3.text: The merit of Josaphat screened him from punishment.
Syriac, "disposed." Faith teaches that we can do no good of ourselves, but that we have free will to choose either good or evil.

19:4.text: Ephraim. This was the northern boundary, as Dan or Emath had been under David and Solomon.

19:6.text: And whatsoever. Hebrew, "who is with you in the judgment." The Lord will confirm your just decisions, and punish your injustice.
God stood in the synagogue of gods, or judges, Psalm 81:1. Judges ought not so much to regard man as God, when they pass sentence.
How then will the Anabaptists deny that a man may sit in judgment?

19:7.text: Gifts. The contrary is the source of so much corruption.
It would be proper to inscribe this sentence on the walls of all courts of judicature, or rather on the hearts of all in office.
None but the true religion can produce such maxims.

19:8.text: And the. Hebrew, "of the Lord, and for disputes among the people among themselves, (Haydock) and they returned, (ver. 4) or dwelt at Jerusalem."
Septuagint, "to judge the inhabitants of," etc. Josephus ([Antiquities?] 9:1.) says, "he returned." Syriac, "And being returned....he exhorted."

19:10.text: Cities. He addressed the judges of the capital, to whom appeals might be made.
Kindred, with respect to inheritances and matrimonial cases. Hebrew, "blood," or murder, whether casual or wilful.

19:11.text: Amarias, (1 Paralipomenon 6:11.) or rather Azarias, the father of Johanan, 1 Paralipomenon 5:9.
Here is a plain distinction between the spiritual and temporal authority, instituted by God, Deuteronomy xvii., and Numbers xxvii.
Masters. Hebrew shoterim, to execute the sentence; (Du Hamel) or "officers."

20:1.text: Year of the World 3108, Year before Christ 896.

Of the Ammonites; people sprung from them. Those who explain the Psalm lxxxii. of this war, find many nations mentioned. Some of these, not daring to appear openly, assumed the name of Ammonites. Perhaps we should read (Calmet) Mánnim.
Septuagint, "the Mineans," who dwelt in Arabia Felix, upon the Red Sea, (Pliny, [Natural History?] 6:28.) or rather the Maonians, in the environs of Gaza.
Some manuscripts and Latin editions have "Idumeans," which is conformable to ver. 10, 23, and St. Jerome, Trad.

20:2.text: Sea of Sodom, (Haydock) or the Red Sea.
Syria, which comprised the country of Ammon.
Perhaps Edom might rather have been in the text, as we find no Syrians in this war, but many of the Idumeans.
Adom and Arom, (Syria. Haydock) have often been confounded, 4 Kings 16:6.
Asasonthamar, "the palm-tree cottage," (Bochart) between Jericho and the Dead Sea.

20:3.text: Juda. The whole kingdom was concerned. Sometimes a fast was proclaimed for one city, 3 Kings 21:9., and Jonas 3:7.
They were accustomed to fast, not only to subdue the flesh, but also for other necessities.

20:4.text: Cities. Here we have an example of a pilgrimage to a holy place, as God is more willing to hear the virtuous in one place than in another, 2 Paralipomenon 6:7., etc.

20:5.text: New court of the priests. Why it is so called, does not appear.
Some understand the court for the women; (Salien) others, that for Gentiles.

Probably some of the courts had been found too spacious after the schism, and had been neglected; but now, as many had returned to serve the Lord in the dominions of Josaphat, he had made some repairs.

20:7.text: All, or the greatest part. Hebrew, "drive out the inhabitants."

20:9.text: Sword of. Protestants, "as the sword, judgment, or," etc. Septuagint, "the sword, judgment, death, famine." All these are sent in punishment of sin.
The sword of the enemy is a judgment of God.

20:12.text: Judge and punish them, as they fight against thee?

Enough. Yet we read that Josaphat had above a million soldiers; and he knew that his father had discomfited a greater army of Zara with half the forces. He thus puts us in mind that no power can stand against God's will; and he endeavours, by humility, to obtain the protection of heaven. The invading army must, however, have been very numerous, to extort these expressions from a potent king, and to have thrown his whole people into such consternation. It could hardly be less than that of Zara; and thus, in the space of a century, above two millions and a half of idolaters perished by the hand of God, without Juda losing a single man, ver. 29., and 2 Paralipomenon 13:17.

20:13.text: Children. Even the little infants were deprived of the breasts, on such emergencies, that their cries might be more affecting.
The pagans of Ninive even made the cattle also fast, Jonas 3:7.

20:14.text: Jahaziel is known only by this event.

20:16.text: If it had been Zin, we might understand the desert of stony Arabia, where Ptolemy places the town of Ziza.
But we may recollect that the army was already advanced as far as Engaddi; (ver. 2,) so that it seems to have lain nearer Jerusalem.
Jeruel, "the fear of God," which name was given to the place, in consequence of the panic with which God struck the enemy.

20:19.text: High. Rejoicing at the promised deliverance.

20:20.text: Thecua, two mile east of Bethlehem, on the road to Jeruel.

Believe. Faith is the foundation of all good works; (Hebrews xi.) but not alone sufficient, as here the people fasted and prayed, and would have fought, if God had not dispensed with them. See Josue xxiii.

20:21.text: Ever. This was usually sung in times of joy, 2 Paralipomenon 5:13. The army seemed thus to be returning victorious.

20:22.text: Ambushments, placed by the Moabites, (Menochius) and Ammonites, (Haydock) against Juda. Some detachments of Mount Seir, perhaps, coming suddenly on them, were taken for foes, and slaughtered.

20:24.text: Tower. Maspha, in the tribe of Benjamin, (Calmet) or some other (Haydock) eminence.

20:25.text: Insomuch. Hebrew and Septuagint, "Three days passed, while they gathered the spoils, because it was great," (Haydock) "without weight," or estimation.

20:26.text: Blessing. Sts. Jerome and Epiphanius place Caphar-barucha, "the field of blessing," near Hebron, on the east. Hither Abraham attended the Lord, when he was going to destroy Sodom.

20:29.text: Israel, as he had lately treated Zara, 2 Paralipomenon 14:13.

20:33.text: Places, erected in honour of God, but displeasing to him, 2 Paralipomenon 17:6.
The good king perhaps attempted to take these also away, but was obliged to desist by the rebellious people.
Hence we find that they are here blamed.
They would not conform exactly to the law, (Leviticus 17:3.; Tirinus) and the king was not able to bring all to perfection.
"It is better to pass over....vices, than to shew which we cannot subdue."

20:34.text: Which. Septuagint, "who wrote a book of the kings of Israel."

20:35.text: Things: the victory over Ammon, etc.

20:36.text: Tharsis; probably Cilicia, (Calmet) or some part of the ocean.
Asion-gaber was on the Red Sea; and ships would not have been built there, to trade on the Mediterranean, 2 Paralipomenon 9:21.

21:1.text: Year of the World 3115, Year before Christ 889.

21:2.text: Azarias. The only difference between this and the former name is, that the younger brother's (Haydock) has u at the end, (Calmet) Azrieu. Protestants and Septuagint, make no difference, which we should nevertheless expect.

Juda. Hebrew, "Israel." Septuagint, Syriac, etc., agree with the Vulgate editions, though most of the ancient Latin manuscripts have Israel. Josephat ruled over the principal tribes. Yet it seems probable, from the versions, that the Hebrew formerly read Juda.

21:4.text: Sword. This cruel policy (Haydock) has been very common in the East.

Israel. They had perhaps opposed his impious plans, animated by his brethren. God presently chastised him with the rebellion of Idumea; and though Joram gained a victory over Seir, (4 Kings 8:21.) he was not able to reduce the nation, being called off by other wars, ver. 16. His own subjects at Lobna, a Levitical city in Juda, also abandoned him. Dreadful evils were denounced in a letter from the prophet Elias, who had been translated to paradise nine years before, and at last the honours of sepulture were denied to the wicked king.

21:6.text: Achab. It is supposed by Jezabel. She might be grand-daughter of Amri, 2 Paralipomenon 22:2. The infamous Athalia is blamed for most of the evils which her husband committed.

21:10.text: Day, when the author lived. See 4 Kings 8:20.

21:11.text: Hebrew, "and compelled Juda." Septuagint, "seduced." Syriac, "dissipated Juda."
He used every art of seduction and violence to introduce idolatry, to the ruin of his kingdom.

21:12.text: Elias. Le Clerc would read Eliseus. Grotius supposes that all passed in a dream. Others think that Elias had written the letter before his removal from the conversation of men, some years before, foreseeing the impiety of Joram, and leaving the letter with Eliseus, to be delivered unto him.
But the most common opinion is, that the prophet wrote it in paradise, (Calmet) and sent it to the king by an angel, etc.
Elias had been taken away in the 18th year of Josaphat, who reigned 25; so he shewed this special care of Joram and his kingdom, so many years after his assumption.
Thus the saints in heaven interest themselves in our defence, 2 Machabees 15:11.
Prophet. Hebrew, "And there came in him a writing of," etc.

21:14.text: Thee is not expressed in Hebrew, but it is in the Septuagint and the king was not only afflicted with illness, but with the losses of his people and family.

21:15.text: By little. Hebrew, "by reason of the sickness, (Haydock) day by day," or in two years time, ver. 19.
He was probably ill so long.
Agrippa and Antiochus were treated in the same manner, (Calmet) with a diarrhoea, (Menochius) or dysentery, (Calmet) the vitals being corrupted.

21:16.text: Philistines; who, it seems, had been obedient since the days of David.
Ethiopians, who lay west of the Arabians, from the Red Sea to the lower Egypt and the Nile, (Calmet) bordering on Madian. There was another Ethiopia to the south of Egypt.

21:17.text: Joachaz, alias Ochozias, (Challoner) or Azarias, in Hebrew, 2 Paralipomenon 22:1., and 6.

The variation of names seems to originate in the mistakes of transcribers, very frequently. If we found in some profane author, that Philip had only one son, Ander-alex, left, and that this son, Alex-ander, succeeded him, we should readily allow that the first syllables had been erroneously placed last, (Kennicott) as on this occasion aez-ieu stands for ieu-aez. Septuagint has here Ochozias; as the other versions have also Ochozias, (chap. 22:6.) instead of Azrieu, (Haydock) a name given to Ozias, king of Juda, when it belonged to the priests, as it here belongs to one of the captains, 2 Paralipomenon 23:1. Sometimes we find Aezie, 4 Kings 9:16. Strange inconsistency! (Kennicott) See 4 Kings 14:21.

21:19.text: Year of the World 3119, Year before Christ 885.

And. Protestants, "And it came to pass that, in process of time, after the end of two years, his bowels fell out, by reason of his sickness: so he died of sore diseases: And his people made no burning for him," etc., (Haydock) not that the body was usually consumed, but no aromatical spices were burned near it, (Sanctius; Tirinus) as in the funeral of Asa, 2 Paralipomenon 16:14.

21:20.text: Rightly. Septuagint, "unpraised." Hebrew, "without any satisfaction;" or, "he departed unregretted," oppressed with illness, and odious to all.

Kings. Joas, Achaz, Achab, and Manasses, were disgraced in like manner, after their death. The Hebrews then shewed their resentment, without fear. The like custom prevailed in Egypt, and kept many within bounds. No person could receive the usual honours of burial, if his accusers could maintain their charge against his character before a court of above forty people, assembled for the purpose. Calumny was severely punished. But the kings themselves were to stand their trial, while their corpse was placed in the porch of the monument, and the priest spoke their funeral oration. The people testified their approbation or discontent, "and many of the kings have been deprived of a glorious and legal burial, on account of the opposition of the multitude."

22:2.text: Forty-two, etc. Diverse Greek Bibles read twenty-two, agreeably to 4 Kings 8:18., (Challoner) with the Syriac and Arabic. The Roman (Calmet) and Alexandrian Septuagint have "twenty."
Ochozias was the youngest son, and his father died at forty years of age, 2 Paralipomenon 21:20.
Others would date from the birth of Amri, (Broughton) or of Athalia. But it is most probable that we should read 22, as [in] 4 Kings.

The contradiction has so much perplexed the commentators, that Walton (prol. 36) puts it among the quaedam apora; and De Dieu says, "I would rather plainly confess that this difficulty is to us inexplicable." The error here, "is plainly owing to a mistake of one of the Hebrew numeral letters," c, being put instead of m, which was formerly more similar than it is now. "In Origen's Hexapla, one of the Greek copies (probably that found in Caracalla's time) reads here cb, by rendering the number 22, eikosi kai duo, all which proofs make the mistake indubitable, and strongly recommend this method of correcting it."
Mariana, Tirinus, etc., had already suggested this plan, (Haydock) which is very plausible.
Ochozias might reign twenty years with his father, and only one alone, 4 Kings 8:26.

22:3.text: Pushed. Hebrew and Septuagint, "was his counsellor to," etc.

22:6.text: Ochozias. So all the versions read. Hebrew, "Azarias," is probably incorrect, unless this man had three names.

22:7.text: For. Protestants, "and the destruction of Ahaziah was of God, by coming to Joram." God had decreed to punish him by the hand of Jehu, 4 Kings ix.

22:8.text: Brethren, who had been themselves slain by the rovers. Ochozias employed his nephews at court, and they expected that he would make some stay at Jezrahel.

22:9.text: Hid in the kingdom of Samaria, (Malvenda) at Mageddo, where he was wounded; though he was perhaps put to death at Jezrahel, 4 Kings 9:27.
Reign. Hebrew, "the house of Ochozias had no one to bear the weight of the kingdom." The children of the deceased were too young, particularly as most of the princes were slain.
Human hope failed, but God's providence kept David's issue on the throne, till Christ appeared.

22:10.text: All, except Joas, (ver. 11., and 4 Kings 11:1.; Calmet) ninety-five years after Solomon's death.

22:11.text: High is not expressed in Hebrew or Septuagint, but he is supposed to have been the pontiff.

23:1.text: Year of the World 3126, Year before Christ 879.; 4 Kings xi.
Seventh. Vatican Septuagint, by mistake, has "the eighth."
Captains, whether Levites, or ancient officers of the crown, who were dissatisfied with the usurper, 4 Kings xi.

23:2.text: Israel, of which Juda formed a part.
At the time when the author lived, Israel was destroyed, so that there was no danger of a mistake.
Jerusalem, on a sabbath-day, and most likely on one of the three great festivals: (Calmet) Salien (the year of the world 3157) thinks at the Passover, to avoid suspicion.

23:4.text: Do. Though God's promise was absolute, human means were to be used.

23:5.text: To the sabbath. That is, to perform in your weeks the functions of your office, or the weekly watches.
Porters. Septuagint, "even to the entrance gates."
House, where Athalia resided, or in the apartments of Joas in the temple.
Foundation. Septuagint, "middle." Syriac, "of the cooks, or guards."
It led from the palace up the steps, ver. 21.
Perhaps it was also called Sur, 4 Kings.
But let. This seems to be transposed out of its place from ver. 6.

23:7.text: Slain. Athalia ventured to enter; but she was alone, and not much feared.

23:8.text: High. Hebrew, "the priest dismissed not the courses."
In case of right and necessity, we see here what the high priest could do, though otherwise he would not interfere.

23:10.text: Altar of holocausts, in the court of the priests, while the people stood in their own court.

23:11.text: Testimony. This is explained in the following sentence, (Haydock) which is not in Hebrew nor in many Latin manuscripts. The Jews understand the phylacteries, Deuteronomy 17:18.

23:13.text: Step. Hebrew hamudo, "his pillar," to which he ascended by steps.
Entrance into the court of the priests; but the tribune stood in that of the people, where Joas was in his royal attire.

23:16.text: A double covenant was made between God and the people, and between the king and his subjects.

23:17.text: Those are wilfully blind who will not distinguish these from the images of Christ and his saints.

23:18.text: David, which had been long neglected, 1 Paralipomenon xxiv.

23:20.text: Upper gate of the guards, at the back or western end of the temple, 2 Paralipomenon 5:5.

24:3.text: Him. Joas, as Protestants (Haydock) and most interpreters understand it, with the Syriac, etc. Joiada was too old to think of marrying again, as the Rabbins would explain the Hebrew, conformably to the Septuagint and Arabic, "himself."

24:3.src: Salien, the year of the world 3766.
24:3.text: Joas did not probably marry two at once.
Amasias was born of Joadan, chap, 25:1.
The high priest considered Joas as his son, being married to his aunt.

24:5.text: Year of the World 3147, Year before Christ 857.
Money. The half sicle, (Exodus 30:13., and 4 Kings 12:4.; Calmet) which at first was required for the tabernacle, and afterwards for the repairs of the temple.

24:6.text: Chief "priest," (ver. 11,) where he is called the first. Hebrew, "the head priest."

24:7.text: Children. All but Ochozias had been slain by the Arabs. The plural is often put for the singular. St. Jerome (Trad.) explains it of the priests of the idols, whom Athalia cherished as her own offspring.

24:10.text: That, etc. Hebrew, "till it was done," according to the law.

24:11.text: From day. Literally, "every day," (Haydock) when the chest was replenished.

24:14.text: And for. Hebrew, "and to offer," such as (Haydock) "mortars," Proverbs 27:22. Eali is thus translated.

24:16.text: Israel, particularly to those of the kingdom of Juda.
His, David's, house, (Menochius) as he had restored the crown to his offspring.

24:17.text: Worshipped. Hebrew, "bowed down to the king, and he hearkened to them."
The Jews say that the princes flattered him with the title of god, as he had been educated in the temple.
They petitioned for leave to re-establish the worship of Baal, (Menochius) and paid greater honours to the king than were becoming.

24:18.text: Idols. Hebrew, "sorrows." Septuagint, "the Astartes and idols."

24:19.text: Them. Calling heaven and earth to witness (Calmet) the unity of God.
These prophets might have been performing their sacred commission ever since the reign of Josaphat; during which time Eliseus, Micheas, Jehu, and others, lived, 2 Paralipomenon 20:14., and 18:27.

24:20.text: Year of the World 8164, Year before Christ 840.

It is not certain whether this was the person whom Jesus Christ speaks, (Matthew 23:35.; Calmet) as St. Jerome believes; (Matthew 23:35.) or our Saviour alludes to one of the minor prophets, (Tirinus) or to the father of John the Baptist. [Luke i.]
Sight. Hebrew, "above," perhaps on the steps between the two courts.
To make. Hebrew, "therefore hath he forsaken you."

24:22.text: Require it.. I commit my cause into his hands.
The vengeance of the Lord was not slack. (Haydock) Raro antecedentem scelestum Deseruit pede poena claudo.

24:23.text: Damascus. Hazael had before invaded Jerusalem, 4 Kings 12:17. The Syrian army, which routed Juda, was comparatively small.
The idolatrous princes now received the due punishment of their crimes, ver. 17.

24:24.text: Shameful, is not expressed in Hebrew. But the putting a king to the torture, (ver. 25.) and upbraiding him with his ingratitude and perfidy, was a great indignity.
He had probably entered into engagements with Hazael, when he prevailed on him to depart, the year before.

Nabuchodonosor treated Sedecias in like manner, 4 Kings 25:6. The author of the Hebrew Trad. among the works of St. Jerome, (Haydock) asserts that the children of Joas were slain before his eyes, while the Syrians reproached him for the unjust murder of Zacharias.

24:25.text: Servants. By whom the man is slain, who had murdered his spiritual father.
Son. Hebrew, "sons." See ver. 7. Some think that the brothers of Zacharias were slain, that they might not attempt to revenge his death.
Kings. These sepulchres are amazingly beautiful and spacious. The doors are six feet high, and formed, like the rest, of solid stone, cut in the most elegant manner.

24:26.text: Moabitess. Septuagint read, "the Ammonite....and Moabite." See 4 Kings 12:21.

24:27.text: And. Septuagint, "And all his sons: for five came upon him," with the rest of the conspirators.
Sum. Hebrew, "the greatness of the burden on him," (Haydock) imposed by the Syrians, (ver. 24) or denounced by the prophets. Mossa is often taken for a prophetic menace.
More. Hebrew, "in the researches." Protestants, "story of the book." Addo entitled his book Modross, 2 Paralipomenon 22:22.

Only some extracts from these works have come down to us. Esdras had them before him. The abridgments have occasioned their loss, as has been the case with many other excellent works. This was the more easy, as writing was not then so common.

25:1.text: Year of the World 3165, Year before Christ 839.; 4 Kings xiv.

25:5.text: Families. The officers were well acquainted with their soldiers; (Calmet) and all were more interested for each other's welfare, (Du Hamel) than they are in the present system.
Young. Protestants, "choice men, able to go."

25:6.text: Thousand. Yet "they were so greatly reduced, but a few years before, that there were left....only fifty horsemen," etc., 4 Kings 13:7.

That might be true, at one time, when the people fled from the king's banners, through fear. But they were still in the country, and God gave Israel a saviour, 4 Kings 13:5. See 2 Paralipomenon 13:3., and 17:19.

Silver. This sum was very small, for so great a multitude. Salien suspects talents of gold are meant, though the soldiers were paid in silver. But the sum was only the hire for a short time; (Menochius) and was not distributed among the soldiers, who were to live on plunder, but went into the king's coffers, (Calmet) or belonged to the officers alone.
The talent of silver was worth 342l. 3s. 9d.

25:8.text: And. Hebrew, "But if thou wilt go, do; be strong; (he speaks ironically) God," etc. Septuagint, "if thou thinkest that thou art strong with these, the Lord," etc. Amasias ought to have known that no dependance was to be placed on Israel, while they despised God, 2 Paralipomenon 15:2.

25:9.text: What will. Syriac, etc., "What then is my crime in having given the," etc.
To the. Hebrew or, "for the army."

25:12.text: Rock, afterwards called Jecteel. Some take it to be Petra, 4 Kings 14:7.

25:13.text: Samaria, the capital, where they had made their complaints to king Joas; and receiving no redress, began to plunder, as far as Bethhoron the lower, which Solomon had repaired, and his successors had retained, though it belonged to the territory of Ephraim, 2 Paralipomenon 13:5., and Josue 18:13.

25:16.text: The very character of prophet, authorized him to give counsel even to kings. Amasias had perhaps obeyed this prophet, (ver. 7) but now his heart was puffed up with victory.

25:17.text: Another, and fight. He supposed Joas had connived at his subjects' invasion, (ver. 13.; Haydock. See 4 Kings 14:8.; Calmet) and he now required all Israel to submit to him.

25:18.text: Thistle. This fable insinuated how much Joas despised the insolence of his antagonist, (Haydock) and Amasias had soon reason to repent.

25:20.text: It. Protestants, "it came of God, that he might deliver them into the hand of their enemies, because they sought after the gods of Edom."
The king was permitted to give ear to evil counsellors.
"Jupiter deprives those of understanding, whom he means to destroy."
The heart is hardened on account of former sins.

25:24.text: Obededom, whose descendants guarded these treasures, 1 Paralipomenon 26:15.
Hostages. Protestants, "the hostages also, and returned to Samaria."
Some think that the kings of Juda had before detained these men.
Others suppose that Joas had taken many nobles, whom he exchanged for their sons. But this is all conjecture.

25:27.text: Year of the World 3194, Year before Christ 810.

25:28.text: David. Hebrew, "of Juda," contrary to the Septuagint, Syriac, etc.

26:1.text: Ozias, called Azarias (4 Kings 14:21.; Calmet) improperly.

26:2.text: Juda, till the reign of Achaz, 4 Kings 16:6. It had revolted under Joram.

26:4.text: Done. And was successful, as long as he attended to the high priest, Numbers 27:21.

26:5.text: God, or who had the gift of intelligence and prophecy from God. Hebrew, "who was intelligent in the visions of God." Others have read birath, with the Septuagint, "in the fear;" or Chaldean, etc., "instructing in the fear of the Lord." The Jews childishly understand Zacharias to mean the king himself, in the early part of his reign. Others suppose the priest, who was slain by Joas, is designated.
But this was rather his son (Tirinus; Cajetan; Calmet) and successor, as well as a prophet, who had therefore a more perfect knowledge of God; though none can comprehend his being.

26:6.text: In. Protestants, "about Ashdod." Septuagint, "he built (or repaired) the cities of Azotus," to keep under the Philistines, who had revolted under Joram, 2 Paralipomenon 21:16.

26:7.text: Gurbaal. Gerara, (St. Jerome, Trad.) or Gebal, Psalm 82:8. Septuagint, "the rock," or Petra, in Arabia, where Stephanus places the Gabalenes.
Ammonites. Hebrew mehunim. Septuagint, "Mineans," (chap. 20:1.; Haydock) upon the Red Sea, (Stephanus) or Mediterranean, (Pliny, [Natural History?] 6:28,) or in Arabia Felix.

26:9.text: Corner. Repairing what Joas had demolished, 4 Kings 14:13.

26:10.text: Wilderness, for the protection of shepherds, 4 Kings 17:9.
