Plains. Hebrew, "valleys, and in the plains husbandmen, and vinedressers in the mountains, and in Carmel; for he loved the earth, or husbandry," like David, 1 Paralipomenon 17:25., etc.
Carmel, means, "the vine of God;" as both the mount in Juda, where Nabal resided, and that on the Mediterranean Sea, were famous for wine. Ozias probably cultivated the former mountain.

26:11.text: The term is commonly understood of one who executes the sentence of the judge. But this employment does not suit a general, (Calmet) though such a one might well command. It many denote any "officer."

26:15.text: Engines. They are here mentioned for the first time.
For the. Hebrew, "for he was wonderfully helped till he was established."
He invented many things for the defence of his kingdom.

26:17.text: Azarias. The prophet, who had hitherto kept the king (Haydock) within bounds, was now no more, ver. 5.

26:18.text: To thee. For usurping spiritual authority, the king was opposed by the high priest; and God confirmed the sentence of the latter, by striking Ozias with the leprosy; on which account, he was not only cast out of the temple, but also lost his kingdom, being obliged to live apart; (Leviticus 13:46,) and after death, he could not be buried with his ancestors.

26:19.text: Forehead. So that it could not be concealed. According to the rigour of the law, such an offender was to be slain, Numbers 3:10., and 18:7. The leprosy was considered as equivalent, and is styled destruction, (ver. 16,) and death, Isaias 6:1. (Menochius) See 4 Kings 15:6.

26:22.text: Prophet. Yet we find little concerning him in the prophecy of Isaias; so that the work has been lost, except what Esdras hath preserved.

26:23.text: Leper. So much was the disease abhorred, (Calmet) as well as the king's late conduct.

27:1.text: Year of the World 3246, Year before Christ 758.

27:2.text: Lord. Like Ozias, to offer incense. He was content with the royal dignity, without arrogating to himself that of the priests.
Transgressed. Hebrew, "acted corruptly," on the heights, 4 Kings 15:35.

27:3.text: Gate, on the east, repairing (Tirinus) and beautifying it so, (Haydock) that Jeremias (xxvi. 10,) calls it New.
Ophel, on the walls of the city, (Calmet) towards the eastern porch of the temple.

27:5.text: Measures, (coros.) Hebrew corim, also, (Haydock) means an "ass;" as this burden was sufficient for one, being equivalent to 30 Roman bushels.
Give him. Septuagint adds, "These things did the king of Ammon bring to him every year, in the first year, and in the second, and the third."
After paying tribute three years, the Ammonites revolted, and were subdued by Joatham, as they had been by his father, 2 Paralipomenon 26:7.

27:7.text: Wars, with Rasin and Phacee, 4 Kings 15:37.
Juda. What concerned both kingdoms was carefully preserved; and Esdras informs us from what records he composed this work.

28:1.text: Year of the World 3262, Year before Christ 742.; 4 Kings xvi.

28:3.text: Benennom, "the son of Ennom," 4 Kings xvi.

28:4.text: Tree. Remarkable for its size and beauty; as the heathens supposed that some deity lay there concealed.

28:5.text: The two kings had besieged Jerusalem without success: (4 Kings) but now their forces were divided.
The motives of this war were unjust,

28:7.text: At the same time, is not in Hebrew (Calmet) or Septuagint.
Zechri, one of the generals of Phacee, slew the king's son, etc., after the engagement.
Next. Joseph and Aman enjoyed the like dignity, Genesis 41:42., and Esther 13:3. The high priests had also seconds, 4 Kings 25:18. Septuagint, "vicar of the king."

28:9.text: Heaven, and cries for vengeance against you. Though God designed to punish his people, your cruelty will not be excused.
The effect of this remonstrance, shews that all the kingdom of Israel was not devoid (Calmet) of feeling and religion.
They set a noble example for Christian princes to follow after a victory.

28:10.text: God, who forbids this, Leviticus 25:43., and Micheas 2:8, 9. Hebrew, "are there not with you, yea, with you, sins against?" etc. If Juda have deserved punishment, reflect if you also be not guilty, ver. 13. Septuagint, "Behold, am I not here with you to bear witness to the Lord?"

28:13.text: Our, is expressed in Hebrew and Septuagint. These people justly confess that they stand in need of pardon, without adding fresh crimes.
The cruel treatment of the people of Juda, and the design to make them slaves, were grievous sins.

28:15.text: Above. Hebrew, "by name." They deserved to be immortalized.
Anointed. This was very common, (Menochius) and refreshing.

28:16.text: Kings. Hebrew, "kings." But Septuagint have the singular, 4 Kings 16:7.

28:17.text: Year of the World 3264, Year before Christ 740.
And. Septuagint, "Because the Edomites had come,...and taken many prisoners." Protestants, "for again," etc.

28:18.text: Gamzo. Syriac, "Garam," perhaps Gaza, (Calmet) or a city south of Juda.

28:19.text: Of Juda. Hebrew, "Israel." But the Septuagint have "Juda."
The names are often used promiscuously.
For he. Septuagint, "because he or it had apostatised from the Lord."
Stript. Protestants, "he made Judah naked, and transgressed sore," etc. Thus were the idolatrous Israelites exposed to shame and misery, after they had adored the golden calf, Exodus 32:25.
In the Bacchanalian riots, the pagans threw off their garments, to shew that they trusted entirely in their false gods.
Achaz had plunged his country in distress, (Calmet) both of soul and body, (Haydock) by his scandalous deportment.
Of all help, is added by way of explanation; (Tirinus) as God would no longer rescue his rebellious people from the hands of the enemy.

28:20.text: And. Hebrew and Septuagint, "And....the king....came against him, and smote him." Hebrew continues, "but strengthened him not" in the end; though he had been invited, (ver. 16.; Haydock) and had actually made a diversion in favour of Achaz. But not being satisfied, he afterwards returned, and made cruel havoc in the country, imposing tribute upon the king, ver. 21., 4 Kings 16:7., and 18:7. It is therefore unnecessary to correct the Hebrew, with Grotius, to make it agree with 4 Kings 16:7. Thus---he "came to his aid, and relieved him from his distress, (ver. 21,) and granted him assistance."

28:21.text: Hebrew, "and gave it to, etc.: but he did not help him."

28:22.text: Wicked policy availeth nothing, but entails much evil.
King. Hebrew, "that king Achaz." The sacred writer thus stigmatizes the impious king, who grew worse the more he was scourged.
This was the monster, which could not be tamed.
Eva. "ille," is five times used as a reproach, and often to signify applause.
It gives us to understand (Haydock) that Achaz acted thus of his own accord.
Septuagint, "And king Achaz said, (23) I will studiously seek the gods of Damascus, who have buffetted me," etc.

This resolution he took while the arms of Rasin were formidable to him: but he put it in execution after the king of Assyria had delivered him, when he went to Damascus to return him thanks, 4 Kings 16:10. This stupid blindness and impiety irritated God so much, that he abandoned Juda to the arms of the king, who had been called in as a protector. How could he expect aid from gods who had suffered their own country and people of Damascus to fall a prey to the invader!

28:24.text: God: first through fear of the Assyrian, (4 Kings) but afterwards he permitted it to continue in the same state of irreligion.
So easily do people fall into this abyss, who begin to neglect instruction!
Ezechias took care to open the temple again, 2 Paralipomenon 29:3.
None of the former kings had dared (Haydock) to prohibit the worship of the true God.

28:27.text: Israel is put as comprising Juda.

29:1.text: Year of the World 3278, Year before Christ 726. + 29:1 4 Kings 28. + 29:1 Abia. The last syllable is neglected, 4 Kings 18:2.
Some assert that she was a descendant of the high priest, who had been stoned.

29:3.text: Reign, in the assembly, which met to recognize his authority.
Them, adorning them with plates of gold, 4 Kings 18:16.

29:4.text: Street, or court, before the eastern gate.

29:5.text: Sanctuary, or temple, ver. 7. The Jews suppose that idols had been placed in the very sanctuary: (Lyranus) but why then were the doors shut?

29:7.text: Sanctuary, or court of the priests, where the victims were slain. The blood of some was taken into the most holy place, on the day of expiation.

29:8.text: Trouble. Hebrew zuae, (Haydock) is rendered vexation, Isaias 28:19. Septuagint, "ecstasy." The Jews were frequently driven from their homes.
There were at a loss what to do, confounded and despised.

29:9.text: So had the wives and family of king Joram been treated, 2 Paralipomenon 21.

29:10.text: Covenant, swearing to observe the law given by Moses.

29:11.text: Negligent. Hebrew adds, "now," when every thing tends to open your eyes.

29:16.text: Cedron, as Josias did with the idolatrous altars, 4 Kings 23:12. The priests brought what was unclean from the inner temple into the porch.
This labour lasted eight days, as the cleansing of the porches had done.

29:17.src: Salien, the year of the world 3309.
29:17.text: Month, Nisan, corresponding with our March, (Menochius) when Ezechias began his reign.

29:19.text: Forth. Hebrew and Septuagint, "We have prepared and purified, behold they are," etc.

29:21.text: Seven. Only one was prescribed for sins of ignorance, Leviticus 4:13, 22.
But the late transgressions were of a different nature; and the king consults his zeal, rather than what he was absolutely obliged to do.
Juda, to expiate the sins of the royal family, of priests, and people.

29:23.text: They laid. The king and princes of the people confessed their sins, Leviticus 4:15.

29:25.text: Prophet. Moses had not required music, except on some occasions, Numbers 10:10. But David acted by God's authority. The institution was designed to promote piety, and a love for religious meetings. Such sacred ceremonies are not to be considered as human inventions, for it was, etc. They command our utmost respect.

29:30.text: Asaph, so famous for music. He had composed some psalms, (Calmet) and twelve bear his name.
But he might only have set them to music, (Haydock) or his band sung them.

29:31.text: Added. Hebrew and Septuagint, "answered," a term used in Scripture, though no question had been proposed.
Filled. You are, in some sense, priests. Protestants, "you have consecrated yourselves." The people brought victims but the priests poured the blood round the altar, ver. 34.

29:33.text: They destined these victims for the use of the temple, (Tirinus) that none might be wanting afterwards.

29:34.text: Holocausts, as the law required, Leviticus 1:6. See 2 Paralipomenon 35:11. The skin might be taken off other victims, by laics.
Priests. Syriac, "The Levites were more timid, or reserved than the priests, to purify themselves."

Both are indirectly accused of negligence, 2 Paralipomenon 30:15. The Hebrew seems to give the preference to the latter, "for the Levites were more upright of heart, (Septuagint, willing) to purify themselves than the priests." But the Alexandrian Septuagint may well agree with the Vulgate. The ceremonies attending the purification of both, may be seen [in] Exodus 29:1., and Numbers 8:6.

The priests had not sufficient time to collect themselves with the purity required, on such a short warning; and the paucity induced the king to put off the Passover till the next month, 2 Paralipomenon 30:3, 15.

29:36.text: Because. Hebrew and Septuagint, "that the Lord had disposed his people, for the thing was done suddenly," (Haydock) to place no obstacle to this sudden change from one extreme to the other, at a time when the king was scarcely established on the throne.
Thus the people of England rejoiced, when the Catholic religion was re-established by the means of queen Mary and cardinal Pole.

But the minds of the people are very fickle. Regis ad exemplum totus componitur orbis. Still the joy may show, that all love for truth is "not extinct," as the Cardinal argued from the people's conduct.

30:1.src: Menochius; Salien, the year of the world 3305.
30:1.text: Year of the World 3278.f* + 30:1 Manasses. The pious king thought he might give a general invitation, without umbrage. King Osee was not so impious as his predecessors, 4 Kings 17:2. Afflictions had made his people more docile. The Jews say (Calmet) that the golden calves had been taken away by the Assyrians, and that the king removed the guards, which had been placed to hinder his subjects from repairing to Jerusalem.
Ezechias writes privately to the house of Joseph, as the people were prouder on account of the royal dignity.

30:2.text: Month. The Rabbins pretend that the king intercalated the second Adar, contrary to the rule and advice of the wise, and the that he ought to have allowed those who were pure to celebrate the feast in due time.
But the Caraite Jews deny this pretended leap-year; and we see that Ezechias acted according to the advice of the princes, and that the Scripture praises his conduct.

Those who had a lawful impediment, were authorized to put off the feast till the second month, Numbers 9:10. The nation was under this predicament, as they had not priests at hand, (Tirinus) nor were they assembled.

30:5.text: Many. None had kept the Phase this year.
But those of Israel had not done it for a long time.
Hebrew, "for they had not long before done according to the Scripture." Septuagint, "the multitude had not done," etc.
Yet, even in the worst times, Tobias, (1:6.) and other zealous souls, contrived to comply with their duty.

30:6.text: King. Hebrew, "kings." Phul and Thelgathphalnasar. The latter had taken away some tribes, 4 Kings 15:20, 29.

30:8.text: Yield. Literally, "give your hands," (Haydock) in sign of submission (Calmet) and fidelity. Septuagint, "give glory."

30:9.text: Brethren. God sometimes spares one for the sake of another.

30:10.text: Zabulon. Aser and Nephthali lay more to the north. But they were also invited, as well as the few who might remain on the other side [of] the Jordan, 1 Paralipomenon 5:26. At least, we find that some of Aser came, ver. 11. Thus those, who had been invited last, came first, while Ephraim continued more stubborn; (ver. 18) and the greatest part derided the messengers, as we still see too frequently verified in the days of the gospel.

30:11.text: Yielding. Septuagint, "were converted." Protestants, "humbled themselves, and came."

30:12.text: Hand; grace (Menochius) and power, to endure such unanimity.

30:14.text: To idols, corresponds with the Septuagint, "the false ones," and is added by way of explanation, unless it be lost in Hebrew. The illegal (Haydock) altars had been set up by Achaz, 2 Paralipomenon 28:24.

30:15.text: At length. Septuagint, "were converted." Hebrew, "ashamed, and sanctified themselves."
The ceremonies of purification for priests were longer, (chap. 29:34.) and the sacred ministers were ashamed to be outdone by the people.

30:16.text: Levites, who received the paschal lambs from the unsanctified.
The law does not require the ministry of the tribe of Levi for this purpose, as each one might kill the paschal victim at home. But the people were not sufficiently purified on this occasion.

At other times, laics killed the victims, if they were clean, Exodus 12:6. After the tabernacle was set up, the priests poured out the blood on the altar; and, in latter ages, they slew the victim according to Grotius. But the texts of Josephus ([Antiquities?] 10:5., and Jewish Wars 6:45. Lat. 7:17.; Haydock) are inconclusive; and Philo repeatedly asserts that, on one day, the law authorizes all the people to sacrifice: and, though he lived at Alexandria, his testimony respecting a fact of public notoriety, is not to be rejected. Only the unclean applied to the Levites on this occasion, and the latter had no more right to sacrifice than the rest.

30:17.text: For. Protestants, "for every one that was not clean, to sanctify them unto the Lord." Septuagint, "not able to be purified to the Lord."
The priests alone continued to pour the blood on the altar, till the destruction of the temple.

30:18.text: Ephraim and....Issachar had not been mentioned before, ver. 18.

Some indulgence was shewn to the tribes of Israel, which had been so long rebellious, for fear lest they should return no more; and because they had shewn a good will in procuring the Levites to slay the victims for them, as that was rightly judged a more sacred action than to partake of the feast. The law forbade, nevertheless, the unclean to approach to any thing sacred, Leviticus 15:31., and 22:4., and Numbers 9:6.

But a dispensation was granted, (Menochius) as the Passover could not be celebrated in any but the first or the second month. The people had come with such eagerness, that they had not time to acquire the purity required.
They were however truly penitent, and God dispensed with them.

30:20.text: Merciful; literally, "appeased." Hebrew and Septuagint, "healed." St. Jerome (Trad.) says, "It was asserted that no unclean person could taste the Phase, but death presently ensued; and they understood that the Lord was appeased, because those who eat did not die."
Hebrew girpa may, however, denote that God "pardoned," or did not impute the uncleanness to the people.

30:21.text: Days. It seems all the observances had been omitted in the first month. The Jews teach, that those who have complied with those prescriptions, which require no particular purity, are not bound to keep the festival for seven days, nor to abstain from leavened bread the second month.
That agreed. Hebrew and Septuagint, "of strength," sonorous, (Menochius) which they played on with all their force, or which sounded forth the divine power.

30:22.text: Heart; encouraged them to bear the fatigue for other seven days.
Lord; being of good dispositions, (Calmet) and able musicians.
Praising. Protestants, "making confession to." But the sense is the same.
During the seven days, unleavened bread and peace-offerings were used, ver. 24.

30:23.text: Joy, though not prescribed by the law.
This we should call a work of supererogation, (Worthington) which gives Protestants so much offence.

30:25.text: Proselytes, who had embraced the Jewish law: the rest were not allowed to partake of the paschal victims, Exodus 12:48.

30:26.text: Israel: as many of the tribes came to join with their brethren of Juda.

30:27.text: Levites. These only applauded the solemn blessings, which were given by the priests, Numbers 6:24.
God is said to reside in heaven, because he there displays his glory to the blessed.

31:1.src: Grotius; St. Augustine, q. in Deut. vii., and 12.
31:1.text: Manasses, whether king Osee consented, or the people of Juda followed the dictates of their zeal, neglecting the regular order of things, which forbids the subjects of one kingdom to interfere thus with those of another.

If Ezechias gave orders, we may suppose that he was assured of his neighbour's consent. Josias (chap. 34:6.) followed his example after most part of Israel was led away captive, and the country obeyed the king of Assyria.

31:2.text: There is a confession of God's perfections, as well as of sin.
The Turks style the palace of their emperor, "the sublime porte."
Camp, or temple. Septuagint, "in the gates, in the courts of the house of the Lord."
Grabe has not in the gates.
We know that they did not sing there, but in the courts, (Calmet) or halls.

31:3.text: Substance. Since David had place the ark in the palace of Sion, it seems the kings had furnished the solemn victims for morning and evening, on all the festivals. Solomon also engaged to do it every day; and his magnificence herein astonished the queen of Saba, 2 Paralipomenon 8:13., and 9:4., and 3 Kings 9:25. Ezechiel (xlv.) assigns revenues to the prince for this purpose; but, as the people were often without any, they paid each the third part of a sicle, to furnish the victims, 2 Esdras 10:32.
The king did not wish to exempt himself from contributing, while he required that the people should support the priests. The troublesome times had greatly impaired the revenues of the temple, etc.

31:4.text: Lord, without being taken off by worldly cares. It would be well if Christian princes would make an adequate provision for the ministers of religion: (ver. 16.; Tirinus) as the law of nature dictates, that those who serve the public should be supported by it.

31:5.text: Honey for the priests, not for sacrifice, Leviticus 2:11. It may include dates, or palm wine. See Pliny, [Natural History?] 13:4.)
Forth; only wheat, barley, the fruits of vine, olive, pomegranate, fig, and palm-trees.

31:6.text: Things, out of which the Levites had to give tithes to the priests.
Vowed. Hebrew, "consecrated to....God, and laid them in heaps."

31:7.text: Them, bringing from Pentecost till the harvest was ended.

31:10.text: Sadoc, being his grandson, (1 Paralipomenon 6:12.; Calmet) or the father of Sellum.
People, with abundance; so that more would belong to the priests.

31:11.text: Storehouses. Septuagint pastophoria. Solomon had prepared such.
But they had gone to ruin, and others might be requisite.
The old granaries were to be repaired.
People brought their first-fruits to the temple: but the Levites gathered the tithes, and gave a share to the priests, and to those who were on duty, 2 Esdras 10:36.

31:13.text: Hebrew, "captain." He is styled first priest, ver. 10.
To whom, etc., is neither in Hebrew nor in the Septuagint.

31:14.text: And of. Protestants, "To distribute the oblations of the Lord, and the most holy things," (Haydock) to the priests.

31:16.text: Besides, (exceptis.; Calmet) not to mention. (Haydock) See Leviticus 23:38. Girls are included, ver. 18. It is supposed (Calmet) that children were at the breast till they were three years old, and therefore no portion is assigned them before.

31:17.text: Upward. Ezechias followed the regulation of David, rather than that of Moses, who deferred the service till the 25th or 30th year, Numbers 4:3., and 8:24., and 1 Paralipomenon 23:24.

31:18.text: To all. Protestants, "the genealogy of all their little ones, their wives, and their sons, and their daughters, through all the congregation: for in their set office they sanctified themselves in holiness."
All complied with their respective duties; the people, the distributors, and the priests.

31:19.text: Aaron. Hebrew and Septuagint add, "the priests," (Menochius) which is sufficiently understood.

31:20.text: God. This is the highest praise that can be given to a prince; and, in effect, we read there was none like him, 4 Kings 18:5.
Septuagint omit and truth. Ezechias loved it, and sincerely desired to serve the Lord.

32:1.text: Year of the World 3291, Year before Christ 713.; 4 Kings 18:13.; Ecclesiasticus 48:20.; Isaias xxxvi.
Truth, and sincere piety of the king, God was pleased to put him to trial; (Tirinus; Menochius; Du Hamel) or, "after these things were established."
Sennacherib attacked Juda when Ezechias had been fifteen years on the throne, and till that time had paid the stipulated tribute, 4 Kings 18:13.

32:1.src: Salien, the year of the world 3319.; Menochius
32:1.text: He now probably refused to pay it any longer.

32:2.text: Jerusalem; as many cities had fallen. The king entered into an agreement with the Assyrian, who soon broke it.

32:3.text: City. The torrent Cedron afforded the only good supply of water. It was often dry, and was only three steps across, when full of melted snow or rain. The waters of Siloe and Gihon were collected in it. Yet it was no very difficult enterprise for the king to turn the stream, and introduce the waters through the rock into a large reservoir, in the city, by the canal, which is mentioned [in] 2 Esdras 2:14., and Ecclesiasticus 48:19. The besieged were more frequently deprived of water, Judith 7:6. Babylon was taken by Semiramis, Cyrus, and Alexander, after they had turned aside the waters of the Euphrates; and Caesar obliged the town of Cahors to surrender, by intercepting the springs.

32:4.text: Land, Cedron, on the east; (Calmet; Menochius) or it may be the Gihon, which supplies Siloe, ver. 30.

32:5.text: Mello, the palace, which was strongly situated, and Ezechias added fresh fortifications, 2 Kings 5:9.

32:6.text: Gate, where there was a room for assemblies, 2 Kings 18:24.
Heart, in the most affectionate and pathetic manner.

32:7.text: More: God and his angels, from whom he expected relief, and was not disappointed, 4 Kings 6:16., and 19:35.

32:9.text: Lachis, whence he sent Rabsaces. Other blasphemous letters were dispatched from Lobna, (ver. 17) when Sennacherib was forced to go to meet Tharaca, 4 Kings 19:9.

32:12.text: His altars. Rabsaces was ignorant of the law, and upbraids the king for a commendable action.
Thus our adversaries foolishly blame us for keeping holy days and abstinence.

32:17.text: He (Sennacherib) wrote, when he was going towards Egypt. Both master and servant employed the same fallacious argument; as if the true God and idols were all upon a level, and as if past success were a sure proof of future victories, ver. 19.

32:21.text: Disgrace. The Jews pretend that he was deprived of his beard and hair, (St. Jerome, Trad.) misapplying the text of Isaias 7:20.
Sword, to prevent their being offered as victims, to appease the idol and the people.
This happened some months after the king's return to Ninive, when he had cruelly oppressed the captive Jews and Tobias, 1:24.

32:24.text: Year of the World 3291, Year before Christ 713.

32:25.text: Up, by riches, and the splendid embassy from Babylon.
Prosperity is more dangerous than adversity.
Few are able to bear good fortune with moderation.

32:30.text: Underneath the walls of the city, making them run westward, 2 Paralipomenon 33:14.

32:31.text: Wonder, at the defeat of Sennacherib; (Grotius; Du Hamel) or rather the retrogradation of the sun, as the Chaldeans studied astronomy with diligence.
Left him, in punishment of his vanity.
Heart. How feeble is man when destitute of the divine assistance!
God permitted, therefore, the king to be tempted, (Calmet) that he might know himself, and that others might have a true idea of him.

32:33.text: Year of the World 3306, Year before Christ 698.
Above, for distinction's sake: (Calmet) meriti praerogativa.
The cell was either higher than the rest, or a sort of pyramid was erected over the tomb of Ezechias.

33:1.text: Year of the World 3306.; 4 Kings xxi.

33:3.text: The host of heaven. The sun, moon, and stars, (Challoner) in imitation of the Phoenicians and ancient Chanaanites.

33:5.src: Salien, the year of the world 3340.
33:5.text: Courts of the Gentiles and of Israel, (Menochius) though afterwards he also placed idols even in the court of the priests, ver. 7.
But we find no express mention of the court of the Gentiles, till after the captivity; (Calmet) so that the two courts mean those of the priests and of Israel.

33:6.text: Valley. Hebrew Ge-ben-hinnom, "the vale of the son of Ennom," (Haydock) a Jebusite, who had formerly possessed that part of the valley of Cedron. (Menochius) Syriac write Gena, whence comes geenna a place at the foot of Moria, and the sink of the city. A constant fire was kept up to burn dead bodies.

33:7.text: Graven. Protestants, "a carved image (the idol which he had made) in," etc. It seems to have been sacred to the grove, (Haydock) or a representation of Astarte, 4 Kings 21:7. The ark was taken away, (chap. 25:3.) to make place for this impure deity, which had four (St. Basil) or five faces, (Eusebius; Salien) that people might adore it on all sides.

33:9.text: Seduced. Using the arts of seduction, as well as open force. Hebrew, "Manasses made Juda....err," 4 Kings 21:16. There is a fund of malice in the human heart, which easily prevails on people to yield, on such occasions, if the grace of God do not support them. Yet, as they are not still without a sense of duty and religion, many will readily comply with the invitations of a pious king, when he earnestly endeavours to reform his people. But these conversions too frequently prove false and unstable, 2 Paralipomenon 29:36.

33:11.text: Captains; probably Tharthan. ([Salien?], the year of the world 3328.) About five years before, Asarhaddon had made himself master of Babylon, to which place the captive king was brought, in the 22d year of his reign, 4 Kings 20:10., etc., and Isaias xxii.)

And carried. Hebrew, "among the thorns, and bound him with fetters (Protestants) of brass; (Junius; etc.) or, "they took Manasses with a hook," insidiously. He might have retired to some desert place, as the Israelites did, to avoid the fury of the Philistines, 1 Kings 13:6.

33:12.text: Distress. "When he had been conducted to Babylon, and cast into a brazen vessel full of holes, over a fire, he called upon all the names of the idols, which he was accustomed to adore; and, as he was not heard, nor set free by them, he recollected what he had often heard repeated by his father: When thou shalt call upon me in tribulation, and shalt be converted, I will hear thee graciously; as it is written in Deuteronomy, (see 2 Paralipomenon 4:29.; Haydock) and his prayer was thus heard by the Lord, and he was delivered and brought back to his kingdom, like Habacuc."

The author of the imperfect work on St. Matthew, (among the works of St. Chrysostom, hom. 1.; Haydock) says, that Manasses was barely allowed as much barley bread, and water mixed with vinegar, as would keep him alive. Whereupon, remembering the Lord, he had recourse to his clemency, and a miraculous flame surrounded him, and dissolving his chains, set him at liberty. Saos-duchin, the successor of Azarhaddon, probably restored him to his throne, some years after his captivity, or perhaps that very year, so that his repentance continued thirty-three years.
Prayed, a proof of the efficacy of sincere repentance.

33:14.text: Without, including what is styled the second city; (4 Kings 22:14,) or he perfected the works begun by his father, or repaired the wall which the Assyrians had thrown down, though we read not of their taking the city.
Fish-gate....to Ophel, from the north-eastern corner, to the place opposite to the eastern gate of the temple, 2 Paralipomenon 27:3.
The fish-gate led towards Joppe, whence the city was supplied with fish.
It was also called David's gate, because it stood near his city. By it, the Chaldeans afterwards rushed in, Sophonias 1:10.

33:16.text: And praise. Protestants, "thanks-offerings." Septuagint, "a victim of salvation and praise."
The sacrifice might be accompanied with canticles, Psalm 26:6., etc.

33:17.text: God. Hebrew adds, "only," and not to idols.

This worship was not superstitious, but it was illegal; and it is a misfortune, that Manasses had not as much influence to guide the people in virtue, as he had formerly, to induce them to follow his example in idolatry.
He could not prevail upon them to destroy the high places.

33:18.text: Prayer, which is not extant in Hebrew, but in Greek and Latin. As yet it is neither received for canonical, nor rejected by the Church.

Israel. That kingdom had been overturned in the sixth year of Ezechias. There was now no need of distinguishing the kings of Juda from those of Israel. The former seemed to have claimed authority over the whole country, 4 Kings 23:19.

33:19.text: But it more probably signifies some particular prophet, (Calmet) Isaias, (St. Jerome, Trad.) "Hanan," (Syriac) or "Saphan."
This work, less than the book of the kings of Israel, is now lost.

33:20.text: Year of the World 3361, Year before Christ 643.
House, in his own gardens, and not with the other kings.

33:22.text: Made, though he had afterwards destroyed them. Amon endeavoured to restore their worship, (Calmet) adoring the same idols, and making other statues in their honour.

33:23.text: Sins, inasmuch as he died in his guilt. Protestants, "but Amon trespassed more and more," (Haydock) following the former bad example of Manasses, Jeremias xv.

33:25.text: Them. Hebrew, "all that had conspired against king Amon."

34:1.text: Year of the World 3363, Year before Christ 641.

34:3.text: The kings of Juda were in their minority, till they had completed their 13th year.
Cleansed. Hebrew and Septuagint, "in the twelfth year he began to purify," etc.
The work was not brought to perfection till six years afterwards, ver. 8.

34:4.text: Idols. Hebrew chammanim, (Haydock) "statues of the sun." The term often denotes those open enclosures, where sacred fire was kept in honour of the sun.

Septuagint, "the heights above them." Syriac, "he destroyed the altars, idols, tigers, and temples, (5) the bracelets, little bells, and all the trees which had been consecrated to idols." Arabic, "the altars, idols, and leopards."

34:5.text: Priests, whom he slew upon the altars, 4 Kings xxiii.

34:6.text: He. Hebrew, "with their swords, (Calmet; Protestants, mattocks) round about;" (Haydock) or, "and in their deserts," the inhabitants being removed. Septuagint, "in their places around." Syriac and Arabic, "in their public places." All the interpreters have read differently from what we find in Hebrew at present. Some may wonder that Josias should act thus, out of his own dominions. But the few Israelites who were left, had placed themselves under his protection; and as he made no ravages, and the Cuthites did not adopt the former superstitious practices of the country, they were not concerned to see the altars destroyed; nor were the kings of Assyria informed of, or interested to stop, these proceedings.

Josias followed the directions of the priests and prophets; (Menochius) and many believe that the Assyrian monarch had given these territories to him, to hold, as his vassal; (Tirinus) or God restored them to the lawful king, (ver. 9) having promised all those countries to the family of David, for the express purpose of keeping up his own worship among his chosen people, and for exterminating idolatry.

34:8.text: Year of the World 3380, Year before Christ 624.
Eselias, or Assia. This man is alone mentioned, 4 Kings 22:3.

34:11.text: Houses, or apartments of the temple, which were gone to decay.

34:12.text: Of music is expressed in Hebrew and Septuagint.
These Levites were chosen, because they were not so constantly employed in the temple, (Calmet) and they were not hired barely to inspect the works, and hence the expense was diminished.

34:13.text: Masters: literally, "master porters." Protestants, "officers and porters;" all Levites.
Jahath, etc., directed them that carried burdens, (Calmet) as the Hebrew intimates; and in general, all the officers were chosen from the same tribe.

34:17.text: Together: literally, "melted down," as the Hebrew and Septuagint signify. Coin was not yet used.
But the pieces of money, of whatever description, were collected.

34:22.text: Hasra. Olda's husband's grandfather is styled Araas, 4 Kings xxii.
Second part of the city, near the temple, or near the second gate, or between the two walls.

34:28.text: Peace. The country enjoyed peace, as Pharao was gone; (Menochius) and Josias did not witness the evils here denounced.

34:33.text: In Israel, who had fled into his dominions. See ver. 6.
They. Septuagint, "he," etc. Josias worshipped God with all his heart: but all Israel paid him, at least, an external adoration.

35:3.text: By whose. Septuagint, "the powerful in Israel, that they might be sanctified to the Lord; and they put," etc. Hebrew, "who instructed all Israel, the holy people of the Lord: Put," etc.
No more. Hebrew adds, "upon your shoulders;" (Haydock) whence it is inferred, that it had been carried about the cities of Juda, under the impious kings, who would not suffer it in the temple.
Some believe that it was kept in the house of Sellum, the uncle of Jeremias, and husband of Olda.

35:5.text: Families. Hebrew adds, "according to the divisions of the families of the fathers of your brethren, the people, (Haydock; or laics. Calmet) and the division of the families of the Levites." All were placed, with great order, in the temple.

35:6.text: Which. Hebrew, "of the Lord, by the hand of Moses," which ordinances he has written down.

35:7.text: In. Hebrew, "for the Passover-offerings, from the flock, lambs and kids, in number 30,000." Et reliqui pecoris, is not expressed.
The small cattle might be intended for other sacrifices.

35:8.text: Rulers: the first alone was high priest.
Small cattle: literally, cattle of different sorts;" commixtim. Protestants supply, "small cattle," which is not now in Hebrew.
Syriac and Arabic have, "sheep;" but we had best follow (Calmet) the Septuagint, "sheep, both lambs and kids." Either would suffice, Exodus 12:5.

35:9.text: Cattle. Protestants again supply these words. Septuagint have, "sheep."

35:11.text: Blood is expressed in the Septuagint and understood in Hebrew.
With. Hebrew and Septuagint, "from their hands;" (Haydock) receiving it from the officers, who might slay their own victims, (Calmet) unless they were unclean, 2 Paralipomenon 30:17.
Holocausts. Hebrew and Septuagint speak of the paschal lambs.
Holocausts were regularly to be flayed by priests, Leviticus 1:6. The hurry of the solemnity, (Calmet) and necessity, here excused the Levites, (Menochius) if we understand proper holocausts.

35:12.text: Separated. Septuagint, "prepared the holocaust to give; having taken notice to whom the victims belonged, before they took off the skin, (Menochius) or rather, separating these from the other peace-offerings.

35:13.text: Victims. Hebrew and Septuagint, "but the holy offerings they boiled." See Exodus 12:9.

35:15.text: Prophets. Hebrew, "seer." But Septuagint, etc., read in the plural. These were "prophets of the king," (Haydock) or masters of music.
Vulgate might insinuate that these three gave orders, as well as David. But they were in office under him, as people of the same name were, probably, under Josias.

35:18.text: As Josias kept. He displayed greater liberality (Estius. See 4 Kings 23:20.) and devotion; and the festival had also been neglected for some time.

35:20.text: Year of the World 3394, Year before Christ 610.
Charcamis belonged to the Assyrian monarch, (Isaias 10:9.) who subdued the empire of Babylon, 2 Paralipomenon 33:11.

35:21.text: With me. Grotius thinks that Jeremias had given the order; and the author of Hebrew Traditions, supposes that he forbade Josias to oppose Pharao, who, it seems, was assured of victory.
But this is uncertain; and Josias might justly suspect that the king of Egypt spoke without authority.
The augurs of the latter might declare the truth, (Menochius) though they could not dive into futurity.
Herodotus (i.) informs us, that Nechos fought against the Syrians, (or Israelites) in Magdelum, and took Cadythis, a city as large as Sardis, of Lydia; probably Jerusalem, (Du Hamel) or Cades.

35:22.text: Prepared. Hebrew, "disguised himself," like Achab; (Calmet) or set his army in array. Septuagint, "he was bent on fighting him."
He supposed that Pharao intended to invade his dominions, and God would withdraw him from the world.

35:24.text: After the manner of kings, is not in Hebrew, etc.
Curtius (iv.) informs us, that the Persian monarch had always a horse behind his chariot, to be ready in case of any accident.

35:25.text: Lamentations. Some think that we have them in the Bible.

35:25.src: Salien, the year of the world 3425.
35:25.text: Others believe that they are lost.
Law, on the anniversary; (Menochius) or when any calamity occurs, the lamentations of Jeremias are used.
There were collections of such poems, as well as of canticles, for victory and marriages.

35:27.text: Israel is placed first in Hebrew and Septuagint.
The kings of Juda assumed the title, as the kingdom had been subverted above a century; and the wretched remains of Israel had retreated into the territories, (Calmet) or acknowledged their dominion.

36:1.text: Year of the World 3394, Year before Christ 610.

36:3.text: Him. Joachaz had attempted to defend himself, and had been conducted prisoner to Reblatha, 4 Kings 23:33., and Ezechiel 19:4. He was now deposed in form, and a tribute laid upon the people.

36:4.text: Brother. From the time of David, the son had always succeeded his father.

36:6.text: Babylon, but sent him back soon after; (Tirinus) unless he altered his first design, (Du Hamel) and slew him disgracefully at Jerusalem.

36:6.src: The year of the world 3402.; Usher
36:6.text: He allowed him to reign as a vassal other three years, but took Daniel, etc., captives. The seventy years of Jeremias, (xxv. 11.) are to be dated from this event.

36:7.text: The vessels. Hebrew and Septuagint, "a part of the vessels;" probably the most precious, (ver. 10) breaking the rest in pieces, as he left none behind, ver. 18.

36:8.src: Denis the Carthusian; R. Sal., etc.
36:8.text: In him. The Jews explain this of the diabolical characters forbidden, Leviticus 19:28.
But it may simply denote the hidden sins of the king, which God will bring to light, or his rebellious conduct.

36:9.text: Eight years old. He was associated by his father to the kingdom, when he was but eight years old; but after his father's death, when he reigned alone, he was eighteen years old, 4 Kings 24:8.
He only enjoyed the throne three months and ten days.
We must however observe, (Haydock) that the Alexandrian Septuagint, the Syriac, and Arabic read here, eighteen.
"It is, in my opinion, a pity that the translators have not mended such apparent errata of the scribe of the present Hebrew out of 2 Kings 24:8., or out of Septuagint, or out of common sense."
These eight years may be dated from the captivity, and not from the king's birth.

36:10.text: Year, in spring, when kings go out to war.
Uncle. Septuagint, "brother of his father," which comes to the same sense, and was certainly the case. Hebrew, "his brother." Abraham indeed gives the title to his nephew, Genesis 13:8.

36:13.text: Year of the World 3405, Year before Christ 599.

36:15.text: Early. Protestants, "betimes, and sending: because," etc.
Like a good master, God (Tirinus) sent his servants to work at day-break, (Haydock) omitting nothing to induce them to return to him, that he might not be forced to punish them with severity.

36:16.text: Remedy. All attempts to convert them proved in vain; (Tirinus) and God, perceiving that they even despised his calls, became inexorable.

36:18.text: Vessels. Only a few remained, to be restored by Cyrus, ver. 19.

36:20.text: Persia; Cyrus, who subdued the country.

36:21.text: Sabbaths, every seventh year, Leviticus 25:2., and 26:43., and Jeremias 25:11., and 29:10.
Expired: not that the earth was quite neglected, all that time, from the third year of Joakim, the year of the world 3398 till 3468.
But it was almost: particularly after the murder of Godolias, (Menochius) the public worship was at a stand, etc.
During this fifth age, the true religion subsisted invariable, not only in the kingdom of Juda, but also in that of Israel. See Worthington.

36:22.src: The year of the world 3468.; Calmet
36:22.text: First. Cyrus had ruled over Persia from the year of the world 3444. He was now emperor of the East.
These two verses are repeated by Esdras, which evinces that he was the author of this work also, (Tirinus) adding them when he restored (Worthington) or revised (Haydock) the holy scriptures.

36:23.text: The Lord, (Jehova.) In Esdras we find yehi, "Let," his God.

Up. The sentence is completed in the following book, which makes it probable that these verses are here inserted improperly, by some transcriber. The book of Chronicles, though for many ages the last in the Hebrew manuscripts, was, perhaps, once followed by Ezra, as in Camb. Manuscript 1., (Kennicott) and as it is now in the Protestant version, taken from the Hebrew.
